question: What is a bull put spread?
human_answers: ["Bull means the investor is betting on a rising market. Puts are a type of stock option where the seller of a put option promises to buy 100 shares of stock from the buyer of the put option at a pre-agreed price called the strike price on any day before expiration day.  The buyer of the put option does not have to sell (it is optional, thats why it is called buying an option).  However, the seller of the put is required to make good on their promise to the buyer. The broker can require the seller of the put option to have a deposit, called margin, to help make sure that they can make good on the promise.   Profit... The buyer can profit from the put option if the stock price moves down substantially.   The buyer of the put option does not need to own the stock, he can sell the option to someone else.  If the buyer of the put option also owns the stock, the put option can be thought of like an insurance policy on the value of the stock.  The seller of the put option profits if the stock price stays the same or rises.  Basically, the seller comes out best if they can sell put options that no one ends up using by expiration day. A spread is an investment consisting of buying one option and selling another. Let's put bull and put and spread together with an example from Apple. So, if you believed Apple Inc. AAPL (currently 595.32) was going up or staying the same through JAN you could sell the 600 JAN put and buy the 550 put.  If the price rises beyond 600, your profit would be the difference in price of the puts.  Let's explore this a little deeper (prices from google finance 31 Oct 2012): Worst Case:  AAPL drops below 550.  The bull put spread investor owes (600-550)x100 shares = $5000 in JAN but received $2,035 for taking this risk. EDIT 2016: The \"worst case\" was the outcome in this example, the AAPL stock price on options expiry Jan 18, 2013 was about $500/share. Net profit = $2,035 - $5,000 = -$2965 = LOSS of $2965 Best Case:  AAPL stays above 600 on expiration day in JAN. Net Profit = $2,035 - 0 = $2035 Break Even:  If AAPL drops to 579.65, the value of the 600 JAN AAPL put sold will equal the $2,035 collected and the bull put spread investor will break even. Commissions have been ignored in this example."]
chatgpt_answers:
["A bull put spread is a bullish options strategy that uses two puts on the same underlying asset: one put is bought at a lower strike price, and another is sold at a higher strike price. It gets the “bull” label because it reflects the expectation that the underlying asset will rise.

The idea is to capture the difference between the two put premiums, with the trade set up to perform best if the underlying asset moves higher. In that case, the lower-strike put should lose value, while the higher-strike put should gain value, allowing the trader to potentially profit by selling the higher-strike put for more than it cost to buy the lower-strike put.

Risk is capped in this strategy. The maximum loss is the distance between the two strike prices, less the premium collected from selling the higher-strike put. Another way to think about it is that the trader’s downside is limited to the amount paid for the lower-strike put.", "A bull put spread is a bullish options strategy in which you buy a put option with a lower strike price and, at the same time, sell a put option with a higher strike price on the same underlying asset. Since both legs are opened together, the trade is meant to benefit if the underlying asset rises.

For example:
You buy a put with a $50 strike for $2 per share.
You sell a put with a $55 strike for $1 per share.
Your net outlay for the spread is the difference between the two, which comes to $1 per share.

If the underlying asset goes up, both puts lose value. The result depends on how the spread’s value changes relative to what you paid. So if the underlying asset climbs to $60 per share, the $50 put might fall to $0.50 per share, while the $55 put could expire worthless at $0. In that case, the profit would be $0.50 - $1.00 = -$0.50 per share.

Bull put spreads are often used to collect income while still maintaining exposure to a rise in the underlying asset. That said, the maximum gain is capped at the difference between the two strike prices minus the spread’s cost. If the underlying asset fails to rise, or drops sharply, the trader may take a loss."]

question: Buying from an aggressive salesperson
human_answers: ["If something in any transaction in life—financial or otherwise—doesn’t make you feel comfortable and the choice is between saving money with one thing versus another, don’t sell your personal needs short. Pay more elsewhere that treats you the way you expect to be treated. In the long run the $$$ you “save” in a cheaper transaction might cost you more in the headaches and annoyance you have to swallow in dealing with this “bargain” in the future. Your question is this: “Do his sales tactics indicate other underlying problems? How can I deal effectively with those tactics?” And you state this as well: “To make a long story short, the dealer's aggressive sales tactics have made me somewhat uncomfortable.” And finally ask: “How can I deal effectively with those tactics?” Okay, first and foremost if you feel discomfort in anything in life—not just a financial situation—just walk away. You might have to say “No…” when doing this but it’s not always the case you will have to counter aggression with aggression. And specifically in the case of a purchase like this, you need to also ask yourself: “Is this discount being offered me worth the headache I am getting?” At the end of the day money is meaningless and has it’s main worth as an economic motivator/stimulator: Someone has a need and someone else has something that can solve that need. What would it take for the side of need to connect to the side of solution to that need? This is the basic concept surrounding all economics. So that said, I have personally avoided buying things for less money and paid slightly more elsewhere for a service experience that made me feel comfortable. At the end of the day, if you feel happy in the transaction it helps in the long run more than—let’s say—the $20 to $40 you “save” by buying from someone else. Also—on the side of customer service—this person’s sales techniques sound like something out of a very old fashioned sales playbook. Nowadays it’s all about relationships and service: The immediate sale is not as important for competent and reputable businesses because they know a better customer service experience will bring people back. So it doesn’t matter how long this guy has been in business: It could be that he’s been in business a long time just because he has been in business a long time. That said—and in the case of musical instruments—maybe this guy is really good at care and upkeep of instruments but has crappy sales techniques. Keep that in mind as well and just push back on their sales methods. For things like musical instruments, people might be jerks on the sales side but in the maintenance and repair side they are great. Will you need to go to them if/when your instrument needs repair? Or you don’t care? At the end of the day, go with your gut. And if your gut says, “No…” then just go somewhere else and spend your money on an item you like from a place that treats you the way you need."]

question: Can I add PMI to my principal balance when I take out a mortgage?
human_answers: ["There are few different types of MI you can choose from, they are: Borrower-Paid Monthly (this is what most people think of when they think MI) Borrower-Paid Single Premium (you may have QM issues on this) Lender Paid Single Premium Split Up-front and Monthly The only way to determine which option will ultimately cost you less is to come up with a time estimate or range for how long you anticipate you will hold this mortgage, then look at each option over that time, and see where they fall. To answer your question about the single-premium being added to your loan, this typically does not happen (outside of FHA/VA).  The reason for that is you would now have 90%+ financing and fall into a new pricing bracket, if not being disqualified altogether.  What is far more typical is the use of premium pricing to pay this up-front premium.  Premium pricing is where you take a lender credit in exchange for an elevated rate; it is the exact opposite of paying points to buy down your rate. For example:  say a zero point rate is 4.25%, and you have monthly MI of say .8%.  Your effective rate would be 5.05%.  It may be possible to use premium pricing at an elevated rate of say 4.75% to pay your MI up front--now your effective rate is the note rate of 4.75%.  This is how a single premium can save you money.  Keep in mind though, the 4.75% will be your rate for the life of the loan, and in the other scenario, once the MI drops off, the effective rate will go back down from 5.05% to 4.25%.  This is why it is critical to know your estimated length of financing."]

question: What forces cause a company to write down goodwill?
human_answers: ["To understand the answer we first have to understand what Goodwill is. Goodwill in a companies balance sheet is an intangible asset that represents the extra value because of a strong brand name, good customer relations, good employee relations and any patents or proprietary technology. An article from The Economist explains this very well and actually talks about Time Warner directly - The goodwill, the bad and the ugly When one firm buys another, the target’s goodwill—essentially the   premium paid over its book value—is added to the combined entity’s   balance-sheet. Goodwill and other intangibles on the books of   companies in the S&P 500 are valued at $2.6 trillion, or 10% of their   total assets, according to analysts at Goldman Sachs. As the economy deteriorates and more firms trade down towards (or even   below) their book value, empire-builders are having to mark down the   value of assets they splashed out on in rosier times. A recently   announced $25 billion goodwill charge is expected to push Time Warner   into an operating loss for 2008, for instance. Michael Moran of   Goldman Sachs thinks such hits could amount to $200 billion or more   over the cycle. Investors have so far paid little attention to   intangibles, but as write-downs proliferate they are likely to become   increasingly wary of industries with a high ratio of goodwill to   assets, such as health care, consumer goods and telecoms. How bad things get will depend on the beancounters. American firms   used to be allowed to amortise goodwill over many years. Since 2002,   when an accounting-rule change ended that practice, goodwill has had   to be tested every year for impairment. In this stormy environment,   with auditors keener than ever to avoid being seen to go easy on   clients, companies are being told to mark down assets if there is any   doubt about their value. The sanguine point out that this has no effect on cashflow, since such   charges are non-cash items. Moreover, some investors take goodwill   write-offs with a pinch of salt, preferring to look past such   non-recurring costs and accept the higher “normalised” earnings   numbers to which managers understandably cling. The largest companies   are thus able to survive thumping blows that might otherwise floor   them, such as the $99 billion loss that the newly formed but   ill-conceived AOL Time Warner, as it then was, reported for 2002. But   the impact can be all too real, as write-downs reduce overall book   value and increase leverage ratios, a particular concern in these   debt-averse times."]
chatgpt_answers:
["Goodwill is an intangible asset that reflects the amount paid above the fair market value of a business’s tangible assets and liabilities. It usually shows up on the balance sheet when one company buys another for more than the fair market value of its net tangible assets.

Several factors can lead to a goodwill write-down, including:

Impairment: If the fair market value of a company’s assets and liabilities falls sharply, the recorded goodwill may need to be reduced. This can stem from shifts in market conditions, changes in the industry, or weaker company financial results.

Restructuring: Actions such as a divestiture or merger can prompt a write-down if they cause a substantial change in the fair market value of the company’s assets and liabilities.

Reassessment: Goodwill must be reviewed each year to confirm it remains properly valued. If that annual test shows the value has changed, a write-down may be necessary.

Changes in accounting standards: Updates to accounting rules can also require goodwill to be written down. For instance, new standards may call for different valuation methods, which could lower the amount reported."]

question: How does 83b election work when paying fair market value at time of grant?
human_answers: ["The tax cost at election should be zero.  The appreciation is all capital gain beyond your basis, which will be the value at election. IRC §83 applies to property received as compensation for services, where the property is still subject to a substantial risk of forfeiture.  It will catch unvested equity given to employees. §83(a) stops taxation until the substantial risk of forfeiture abates (i.e. no tax until stock vests) since the item is revocable and not yet truly income.  §83(b) allows the taxpayer to make a quick election (up to 30 days after transfer - firm deadline!) to waive the substantial risk of forfeiture (e.g. treat shares as vested today). The normal operation of §83 takes over after election and the taxable income is generally the value of the vested property minus the price paid for it.  If you paid fair market value today, then the difference is zero and your income from the shares is zero. The shares are now yours for tax purposes, though not for legal purposes.  That means they are most likely a capital asset in your hands, like other stocks you own or trade.  The shares will not be treated as compensation income on vesting, and vesting is not a tax matter for elected shares. If you sell them, you get capital gain (with tax dependent on your holding period) over a basis equal to FMV at the election.  The appreciation past election-FMV will be capital gain, rather than ordinary income.  This is why the §83(b) election is so valuable.  It does not matter at this point whether you bought the restricted shares at FMV or at discount (or received them free) - that only affects the taxes upon §83(b) election."]
chatgpt_answers:
["An 83(b) election is a tax choice available to someone who gets restricted stock. It lets the recipient report the stock’s fair market value as taxable income when the shares are granted, instead of deferring taxation until the restrictions expire. Making the election can lower, and in some cases remove, the tax that would otherwise arise when the stock vests and becomes unrestricted.

To be valid, the election has to be made by filing a written notice with the Internal Revenue Service (IRS) within 30 days after the restricted stock is received. That notice must list the individual’s name and address, the company’s name and address, a description of the stock, and the date the stock was transferred.

When the recipient pays fair market value at the time of grant, that payment is viewed as taxable compensation. In that case, the amount paid must be included in taxable income in the year the stock is received, whether or not an 83(b) election is filed."]

question: Can a retail trader do bid-ask spread scalping through algo-trading?
human_answers: ["In US public stock markets there is no difference between the actions individual retail traders are \"permitted\" to take and the actions institutional/corporate traders are \"permitted\" to take.  The only difference is the cost of those actions.  For example, if you become a Registered Market Maker on, say, the BATS stock exchange, you'll get some amazing rebates and reduced transaction prices; however, in order to qualify for Registered Market Maker status you have to maintain constant orders in the book for hundreds of equities at significant volumes.  An individual retail trader is certainly permitted to do that, but it's probably too expensive. Algorithmic trading is not the same as automated trading (algorithmic trading can be non-automated, and automated trading can be non-algorithmic), and both can be anywhere from low- to high-frequency.  A low-frequency automated strategy is essentially indistinguishable from a person clicking their mouse several times per day, so: no, from a legal or regulatory perspective there is no special procedure an individual retail trader has to follow before s/he can automate a trading strategy.  (Your broker, on the other hand, may have all sorts of hoops for you to jump through in order to use their automation platform.) Last (but certainly not least) you will almost certainly lose money hand over fist attempting bid-ask scalping as an individual retail trader, whether your approach is algorithmic or not, automated or not.  Why?  Because the only way to succeed at bid-ask scalping is to (a) always be at/near the front of the queue when a price change occurs in your favor, and (b) always cancel your resting orders before they are executed when a price change occurs against you.  Unless your algorithms are smarter than every other algorithm in the industry, an individual retail trader operating through a broker's trading platform cannot react quickly enough to succeed at either of those.  You would have to eschew the broker and buy direct market access to even have a chance, and that's the point at which you're no longer a retail trader. Good luck!"]
chatgpt_answers: ["Yes. A retail trader can use algo-trading to scalp the bid-ask spread. Scalping is a strategy built around extracting tiny price movements and converting them into gains. When applied to the bid-ask spread, that usually means buying at the ask and then selling almost immediately at the bid, aiming to pocket the gap between the two. With algorithmic trading, this can be done automatically, so the trader can submit a large number of orders in a short time without manually placing each one. That said, scalping is a high-frequency approach that demands a strong tolerance for risk and the ability to respond fast to changing market conditions. It won’t be a good fit for everyone."]

question: How to calculate the number of months until a loan is paid off (given principal, APR and payment amount)?
human_answers: ["Here is the derivation of the formula, with The loan is equal to the sum of the repayments discounted to present value."]

["To estimate how many months it will take to pay off a loan, use this equation:

number of months = (principal * (APR / 12)) / (payment amount - (APR / 12))

Where:
principal = the loan’s original balance, in dollars
APR = the yearly interest rate, expressed as a decimal
payment amount = the regular monthly payment, in dollars

This formula assumes a fixed APR and an unchanged monthly payment.

For example, if you take out $10,000 at an APR of 0.05 (5%) and make $300 payments each month, the setup is:

number of months = ($10,000 * (0.05 / 12)) / ($300 - (0.05 / 12))

That comes out to about 41.7 months.

Note: this is only an estimate, since it ignores any extra fees or other charges that could be added to the loan."]

question: How do small cap stocks perform vs. large cap stocks (like Dow constituents) during bear trends?
human_answers: ["To a certain extent, small cap companies will in general follow the same trends as large cap companies. The extent of this cointegration depends on numerous factors, but a prime reason is the presence of systemic risk, i.e. the risk to the entire market. In simple terms, sthis is the risk that your portfolio will approach asymptotically as you increase its diversification, and it's why hedging is also important.  That being said, small cap businesses will, in general, likely do worse than large cap stocks, for several reasons. This was/is certainly the case in the Great Recession.  Small cap businesses have, on average, higher betas, which is a measure of a company's risk compared to the overall market. This means that small cap companies, on average outperform large cap companies during boom times, but it also means that they suffer more on average during bear times. The debate over whether or not the standard beta is still useful for small cap companies continues, however. Some economists feel that small cap companies are better measured against the Russell 2000 or similar indexes instead of the S&P 500.  Small cap companies may face problems accessing or maintaining access to lines of credit. During the Great Recession, major lenders decreased their lending to small businesses, which might make it harder for them to weather the storm.  On a related point, small businesses might not have as large an asset base to use as collateral for loans in bad times. One notable large cap company that used its asset base to their advantage was Ford, which gave banks partial ownership of its factories during hard times. This a) gave Ford a good amount of cash with which to continue their short-term operations, and b) gave the banks a vested interest in keeping Ford's lines of credit open. Ford struggled, but it never faced the financial problems of GM and Chrysler.  Despite political rhetoric about Main Street vs. Wall Street, small businesses don't receive as much government aid in times of crisis as some large cap companies do. For example, the Small Business Lending Fund, a brilliant but poorly implemented idea in 2010, allocated less than $30 billion to small businesses. (The actual amount loaned was considerably less). Compare that to the amounts loaned out under TARP. Discussions about corporate lobbying power aside, small businesses aren't as crucial to the overall stability of the financial system Small businesses don't always have the manpower to keep up with changes in regulation. When the Dodd-Frank Act passed, large banks (as an example), could hire more staff to understand it and adapt to it relatively easily; small banks, however, don't always have the resources to invest in such efforts.  There are other reasons, some of which are industry-specific, but these are some of the basic ones. question: Why could rental costs for apartments/houses rise while buying prices can go up and down?
human_answers: ["Economically, you would say that purchased and rented real estate are not perfect substitutes--they are largely separate markets.  Only a few people are able to easily switch from one to the other and that choice is sticky--for example, once you buy a house, prices would have to rise a lot for it to be worth it to sell it and move into an apartment. In both markets there is a supply and demand curve, but the slope of the demand curve for houses to purchase is much steeper than the demand curve for rentals. The market for new housing fluctuates rapidly because it requires a large change in housing prices to change the number of people looking to buy a house.  Most decisions to buy a house are not driven by the state of the housing market.  This describes a supply/demand graph with a very steep demand curve.  Additionally, because of the leverage provided by mortgages, the demand for houses depends critically on relatively small changes in the interest rate and availability of loans.  Thus the steep demand curve shifts all over the place as borrowing conditions change. On the other hand, apartment prices are more stable because people easily move from one apartment to another and people living in their parent's basements easily move into apartments if prices change.  A small change in the price or quantity of rentals brings about reasonable response in quantity demanded.  This is the situation where the demand curve is shallow.  In addition, rentals are not tied to interest rates tightly, nor are they as strongly tied to economic conditions (in a recession, people avoid buying but renters continue to rent)."]
chatgpt_answers:
["Rent and home-sale prices are shaped by different forces. One of the biggest drivers of rent is how many people are trying to lease in a given area: if demand is strong, landlords can often raise rents because tenants are competing for a limited supply of units. If demand eases, they may have to cut rents to avoid higher vacancy rates.

Rents can also rise when the cost of owning and running a rental property goes up. Higher property taxes, more expensive utilities, and costlier repairs can all prompt landlords to charge more so they can cover those expenses.

Home prices, on the other hand, tend to move with a broader set of factors, including:

The overall housing market: When buyer demand is high and inventory is scarce, prices usually climb. If demand weakens or there are lots of homes for sale, prices can drop.

Interest rates: Lower rates make borrowing cheaper, which can bring in more buyers and support higher prices. Higher rates make mortgages more expensive, cool demand, and can put downward pressure on prices.

Economic conditions: A healthy economy generally lifts housing demand and can push prices up. In a weaker economy, demand may soften and prices may fall.

Location: Some cities and neighborhoods are more attractive than others, so homes there often command higher prices. School quality, job opportunities, and neighborhood appeal all play a role.

So rental costs and purchase prices can diverge because they respond to overlapping but not identical pressures. That’s why rents may continue rising even while home prices move up and down."]

question: Problems with Enterprise Value and better valuation techniques
human_answers: ["This is an example from another field, real estate. Suppose you buy a $100,000 house with a 20 percent down payment, or $20,000, and borrow the other $80,000. In this example, your \"equity\" or \"market cap\" is $20,000. But the total value, or \"enterprise value\" of the house, is actually $100,000, counting the $80,000 mortgage.  \"Enterprise value\" is what a buyer would have to pay to own the company or the house \"free and clear,\" counting the debt."]
chatgpt_answers:
["Enterprise value, or EV, is a way to estimate a company’s worth by starting with market capitalization and then adding debt, minority interests, and preferred stock. It is often seen as more useful than market cap alone because it gives a fuller view of the firm’s capital structure.

Still, EV has notable flaws as a valuation metric. It ignores cash and cash equivalents, even though those balances can be substantial. Since EV counts debt but leaves out cash, a company with a higher EV may actually have less net debt than one with a lower EV.

Another limitation is that EV does not reflect the cost of capital, meaning the return investors expect from their money. As a result, a company with a higher EV can still be less appealing than a lower-EV company if its cost of capital is higher.

EV also leaves out growth expectations, even though growth is often a key driver of value. A business with stronger projected growth may warrant a higher valuation than a similar company with the same EV but a weaker outlook.

Other valuation tools try to cover some of these gaps. Discounted cash flow, or DCF, is widely used because it takes future cash flows and the cost of capital into account. Net asset value, or NAV, is another method that focuses on net assets such as cash, investments, and tangible assets.

Because no valuation method is perfect on its own, it is usually best to combine several approaches to get a more rounded view of a company’s value."]

question: How does conversion of Secured Convertible Notes work?
human_answers: ["Let's assume that the bonds have a par value of $1,000.  If conversion happens, then one bond would be converted into 500 shares.  The price in the market is unimportant.  Regardless of the share price in the market, the income per share would be increased by the absence of $70 in interest expense.  It would be decreased by the lost tax deduction.  It would be further diluted by the increase in 500 shares.  Likewise, the debt would be extinguished and the equity section increased.  Whether it increased or decreased on a per share basis would depend upon the average amount paid in per share in the currently existing structure, adjusted for changes in retained earnings since the initial offering and for any treasury shares. There would be a loss in value, generally, if it is trading far from $2.00 because it would be valued based on the market price.  Had the bond not converted, it would trade in the market as a pure bond if the stock price is far below the strike price and as an ordinary pure bond plus a premium if near enough to the strike price in a manner that depends upon the time remaining under the conversion privilege.   I cannot think of a general case where someone would want to convert below strike and indeed, barring a very strange tax, inheritance or legal situation (such as a weird divorce), I cannot think of a case where it would make sense.  It often does not make sense to convert far from maturity either as the option premium only vanishes well above $2.  The primary case for conversion would be where the after-tax dividend is greater than the after-tax interest payment."]

["Secured convertible notes (SCNs) are debt securities that may later be turned into equity, usually after certain trigger conditions are satisfied. If the investor decides against converting, the notes ordinarily reach maturity at the end of the stated term, and the investor gets back the original principal plus any interest that has accrued.

Because SCN terms can differ a lot from issuer to issuer, it’s essential to review the exact note carefully before investing."]

question: What are the typical repayment plans for Credit Cards in the United States?

question: Are stock prices purely (or mostly) only based on human action?

The renowned professor Benjamin Graham once wrote: In the short run, the market is a voting machine but in the long run it is a weighing machine. For example, people are really big fans of tech stocks right now, which suggests why LinkedIn (stock: LNKD) has such a high share price despite its modest earnings (relative to valuation). question: Buying a home with down payment from family as a “loan”

Joel Greenblatt, an American hedge fund manager and author, created a stock-picking approach known as the “magic formula” for finding undervalued companies. It focuses on two main measures:

Return on capital (ROC): This is EBIT divided by invested capital (IC). A higher ROC means the company is using its capital more productively to create profits.

Earnings yield (EY): This is EBIT divided by enterprise value (EV). A higher earnings yield suggests the company may be more undervalued.

With the magic formula, you calculate ROC and EY for each company and rank them on both metrics. The firms that come out strongest on ROC and also have the highest EY move to the top of the list.

The underlying idea is that businesses that are both efficient with capital and inexpensive relative to value may deliver above-average returns. Still, this is only one method for screening undervalued stocks, and there is no assurance it will beat other strategies. question: Do governments support their own bonds when their value goes down?
human_answers: ["Without getting to hung-up on terminology here, the management of a company will often attempt to keep stock prices high because of a number of reasons: Ideally companies keep prices up through performance.  In some cases, you'll see companies do other things spending cash and/or issuing bonds to continue to pay dividends (e.g. IBM), or spending cash and/or issuing bonds to pay for stock buybacks (e.g. IBM).  These methods can work for a time but are not sustainable and will often be seen as acts of desperation.  Companies that have a solid plan for growth will typically not do much of anything to directly change stock prices. Bonds are a bit different because they have a fairly straight-forward valuation model based on the fact that they pay out a fixed amount per month.  The two main reason prices in bonds go down are: The key here is that bonds pay out the same thing per month regardless of their price or the price of other bonds available.  Most stocks do not pay any dividend and for much of those that do, the main factor as to whether you make or lose money on them is the stock price. The price of bonds does matter to governments, however.  Let's say a country successfully issued some 10 year bonds last year at the price of 1000.  They pay 1% per month (to keep the math simple.)  Every month, they pay out $10 per bond.  Then some (stupid) politicians start threatening to default on bond payments.  The bond market freaks and people start trying to unload these bonds as fast as they can.  The going price drops to $500.  Next month, the payments are the same.  The coupon rate on the bonds has not changed at all.  I'm oversimplifying here but this is the core of how bond prices work. You might be tempted to think that doesn't matter to the country but it does.  Now, this same country wants to issue some more bonds.  It wants to get that 1% rate again but it can't.  Why would anyone pay $1000 for a 1% (per month) bond when they can get the exact same bond with (basically) the same risks for $500?  Instead they have to offer a 2% (per month) rate in order to match the market price. A government (or company) could in fact put money into the bond market to bolster the price of it's bonds (i.e. keep the rates down.)  The problem is that if you are issuing bonds, it's generally (caveats apply) because you need cash that you don't have so what money are you going to use to buy these bonds?  Or in other words, it doesn't make sense to issue bonds and then simply plow the cash gained from that issuance back into the same bonds you are issuing.  The options here are a bit more limited.  I have to mention though that the US government (via a quasi-governmental entity) did actually buy it's own bonds.  This policy of Quantitative Easing (QE) was done for more complicated reasons than simply keeping the price of bonds up."]

question: How to understand the caculation of interest for credit cards?
human_answers: ["Suppose you have been paying interest on previous charges in the past. Your monthly statement is issued on April 12, and (since you just received your income tax refund), you pay it off in full on April 30.  You don't charge anything to the card at all after April 12.  Thus, on April 30, your credit card balance  shows as zero since you just paid it off.  But your April 12 statement billed you for interest only till April 12.  So, on May 12, your next monthly bill will be for the interest for your nonzero balance from April 13 through April 30. Assuming that you still are not making any new charges on your card and pay off the May 12 bill in timely fashion, you will finally have a zero bill on June 12. What if you charge new items to your credit card after April 12? Well, your balance stopped revolving on April 30, and that's when interest is no longer charged on the new charges. But you do owe interest for a charge on April 13 (say) until April 30 when your balance is no longer revolving, and this will be added to your bill on May 12. Purchases made after April 30 will not be charged interest unless you fall off the wagon again and don't pay your May 12 bill in full by the due date of the bill (some time in early June)."]
chatgpt_answers:
["Credit card interest is usually figured using a daily or monthly periodic rate, which is then charged on the portion of the balance that is still unpaid. That periodic rate is just the APR broken down into a smaller time period; APR is the annual rate shown for the card.

For instance, if a card carries a 15% APR and interest is assessed monthly, the periodic rate comes out to 1.25% (15% divided by 12 months). If the balance is $1,000 and interest is calculated daily, the daily periodic rate would be 0.00410959% (1.25% divided by 30 days).

To calculate the interest on the balance, multiply the periodic rate by the amount owed. So with a $1,000 balance and a daily periodic rate of 0.00410959%, the interest for that day would be $0.41 (0.00410959% × $1,000).

Keep in mind that card issuers do not all use the same approach, and some apply separate rates to certain transactions like cash advances or balance transfers. It’s wise to review your card’s terms and conditions closely so you understand exactly how interest is charged and what can affect the rate."]

question: What does it mean to long convexity of options?

["Being long convexity in options effectively means you’re bullish on the underlying asset’s volatility. In other words, you’re looking for a sizable price move and hoping to profit from it.

In options trading, convexity describes how an option’s price changes as the underlying asset moves. A long convexity position is one where the options you own usually gain value as the magnitude of the price swings increases. For instance, if you’re long a call on a stock and the stock begins making a sharp move, that call will typically become more valuable.

It’s also worth distinguishing this from being long volatility. Long volatility means you expect volatility itself to rise, while long convexity means you’re using options in a way that allows you to benefit from that kind of price movement.", "Convexity refers to the curvature in the price-yield relationship for bonds. Because bond prices and yields usually move in opposite directions, that relationship is curved rather than linear.

To be “long convexity” is to hold something with a strong degree of that curvature in the way its price responds. In bonds, that would mean a security that rises a lot when yields fall and drops a lot when yields rise.

In options, long convexity simply means being the option buyer. The holder has the right, but not the duty, to buy or sell the underlying asset at a set strike price on or before expiration. This position is generally attractive when you expect a big move in the underlying price. For instance, if you are long convexity through a call option on a stock, you are basically betting that the stock will climb sharply before expiration. If it does, you can exercise the option and buy the stock at the strike price, typically below the market price, and capture the difference."]

question: Investing: P/E Ratio basic question
human_answers: ["While on the surface it might not make sense to pay more than one dollar to get just one dollar back, the key thing is that a good company's earnings are recurring each year. question: How does a high share price benefit a company when it is raising funds?

question: How will interest rate changes affect my government bonds ETF?

question: Could ignoring sunk costs be used to make an investment look more attractive when it's really not?
human_answers: ["I'm not sure that you're considering all the options.   So you may not subtract $X from B, but you do compare NPV(B) to $Y.   Also, remember that we're not trying to figure out the return on B.  We're trying to figure out what to do next.  In terms of planning, the sunk cost is irrelevant.  But in terms of calculating return, A was a turkey.  And to calculate the return, we would include $X in our costs for B.  And for the second option, we'd subtract $X from $Y (may be negative).   Sunk costs are irrelevant to planning, but they are very relevant to retrospective analysis.  Please don't confuse the two.  When looking back, part of the cost for B will be that $X.  But in the middle, after paying $X and before starting B, the $X is gone.  You only have the building and have to make your decision based on the options you have at that moment.   You will sometimes hear $Y called the opportunity cost of B.  You could sell out for $Y or you could do B.  You should only do B if it is worth more than $Y.   The sunk cost fallacy would be comparing B to $X.  Assuming $Y is less than $X, this would make you not do B when it is your best path forward from that moment.  I.e. $Y < NPV(B) < $X means that you should do the project.  You will lose money (apparently that's a foregone conclusion), but you will lose less money than if you just sold out.   You should also do B if $Y < $X < NPV(B) or $X < $Y < NPV(B).  In general, you should do B any time $Y < NPV(B).  The only time you should not do B is if NPV(B) < $Y.  If they are exactly equal, then it doesn't matter financially whether you do B or not."]

question: What causes thrombocytopenia?I'm 17 years old (150 lbs, 5'6") and was diagnosed with thrombocytopenia about two weeks ago.  I'm also suffering from mild depression. Is there any vitamins or supplements I can take to increase the platelet count? Upon admission into the hospital it was 50, then 40, 30 and a week ago, 75. They haven't yet figured out what is causing this but I am curious myself. Thanks.
human_answers: ["hi, there are many causes of thrombocytopenia. most common cause is simple viral infection. many other infection like malaria dengue also causes thrombocytopenia. peripheral destruction or decreased production may be responsible for that. so kindly investigate for the cause and then take treatment accordingly. you will definitly get benifits. thanks for using health care magic."]
chatgpt_answers:
["Thrombocytopenia means the blood has too few platelets. These are the tiny blood cells that help form clots after an injury. A typical platelet count is about 150,000 to 450,000 platelets per microliter of blood. When the count falls under 150,000, it is considered low, and the chance of bleeding can go up.

There are a number of possible reasons for thrombocytopenia, such as:

Infections: viral or bacterial infections may reduce platelet production or make platelets get destroyed faster.

Medications: some medicines, including heparin, aspirin, and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs), can cause thrombocytopenia.

Autoimmune disorders: conditions such as lupus or rheumatoid arthritis can prompt the immune system to make antibodies that attack platelets.

Cancer: certain cancers, like leukemia and lymphoma, may lower platelet counts by interfering with platelet production or speeding up platelet destruction.

Nutritional deficiency: a lack of nutrients such as iron or folic acid can play a role in a low platelet count.

Pregnancy: in some pregnant women, thrombocytopenia can occur because the body’s need for platelets increases during pregnancy.

Alcohol abuse: heavy alcohol use can also reduce platelet counts.

It’s important to keep working with your healthcare provider to find the exact cause of the low platelet count and get the right treatment. In some situations, vitamin or mineral supplements may help raise platelets, but they should only be taken with guidance from a healthcare provider."]

question: Q. How to manage nosebleed?Hi doctor,My 3 year old daughter has had three nosebleeds in about 3 hours. During the nosebleeds, she said her cheek bone area close to her nose hurts. What to do?

This keeps blood from running down the throat, which can lead to nausea or vomiting. Next, pinch the soft part of her nose, just below the bony bridge. This applies pressure to the bleeding vessels and may help the bleeding stop. Keep the pressure on for at least 5–10 minutes.

If it is still bleeding after 10 minutes, you can use a saline nasal spray or put a thin layer of petroleum jelly just inside the nostrils. Both can help keep the lining of the nose moist and may lower the chance of more nosebleeds. If the bleeding does not stop, or if she continues to have repeated nosebleeds, get in touch with her healthcare provider. You should also seek medical advice if she develops symptoms like fever, headache, or facial pain.

For now, it may help to increase moisture in the air at home with a humidifier, or even a bowl of water near a heat source, because dry air can make nosebleeds more likely. I hope this helps, and if you’re still worried or have more questions, please contact your healthcare provider."]

The ultrasound report, your child’s symptoms, and his past medical history all matter as well. Interpretation is also limited without the lab’s own reference ranges, since those can differ a little from one laboratory to another. Normal ranges may also vary with age and sex.

In general, the tests you mentioned are used in this way:

Alkaline phosphatase (ALP): an enzyme found in the liver, bones, and small intestine. A raised level can be seen with liver or bone conditions.  
Alanine transaminase (ALT): a liver enzyme. An increased value may suggest liver injury or disease.  
Aspartate transaminase (AST): present in the liver, heart, muscle, and other tissues. A higher level can also point to liver damage or disease.  
Total bilirubin: a byproduct of red blood cell breakdown. If elevated, it can be linked to liver or blood disorders.  
Direct bilirubin: a fraction of bilirubin. When this is raised, it may indicate liver disease.  
Gamma-glutamyltransferase (GGT): an enzyme in the liver, pancreas, and bile ducts. Higher levels can suggest liver or bile duct disease.  
Albumin: a protein made by the liver. Low levels may be seen with liver disease or poor nutrition.

The most appropriate next step is to go over these results with your child’s doctor, who can interpret them alongside the ultrasound findings and the full clinical picture."]

question: how do forensic auditors examine financial reporting

Checking the company’s financial statements and related reports for inconsistencies or warning signs.

Speaking with management and staff to learn how financial processes work and to look for suspicious conduct.

The formation usually involves several stages:

Deposition: Weathering and erosion wear down older rocks and minerals into sediment. Wind, water, or ice then transport that sediment and deposit it in a new place.

Compaction: As more sediment accumulates on top, the pressure from the upper layers squeezes the lower layers, making them denser and more tightly packed.

Cementation: Minerals within the sediment can dissolve and later crystallize again, serving as a natural binder that holds the grains together.

Common sedimentary rocks include sandstone, shale, and limestone, and each forms from different sediments under different conditions.", "Sedimentary rock develops when sediment builds up in layers over long periods of time. The main kinds of sedimentary rocks are:

Clastic sedimentary rocks: These come from the accumulation and cementing of clasts, or fragments of broken rock. Sandstone, shale, and conglomerate are examples.

Chemical sedimentary rocks: These form when minerals dissolved in water come out of solution and gather together. Examples include rock salt, limestone, and dolomite.

Organic sedimentary rocks: These form from collected organic material, such as shells from marine organisms or plant remains. Coal and limestone are examples.

Deposition: Sediment is carried by wind, water, or ice and then laid down. Over time, it becomes compacted and cemented into solid rock.

Diagenesis: Even after lithification, the rock can keep changing chemically, including through mineral precipitation, which may change its texture and composition. This later phase is called diagenesis.

It also affects characteristics of an atom’s electron cloud, including electron energy levels and the shapes of atomic and molecular orbitals. By understanding effective nuclear charge, chemists can better predict how atoms and molecules will behave, which matters in areas such as medicine, materials science, and environmental science.", "Effective nuclear charge is a chemistry idea describing the net positive pull an electron feels inside an atom or molecule. In other words, it is the charge an electron effectively experiences once the attraction from the nucleus and the repulsion or shielding from other electrons are both considered.

The concept matters because it helps account for and predict electron behavior in atoms and molecules. For instance, the effective nuclear charge acting on an electron can affect its energy and how readily it takes part in chemical reactions.

It is usually estimated by combining quantum mechanical ideas with experimental data. Several factors can change the effective nuclear charge for a particular electron, such as the influence of neighboring electrons, shielding by inner electrons, and the overall size of the atom or molecule.

It takes into account shielding from the inner electrons, which weakens the full nuclear charge experienced by electrons farther out.

This matters because it controls how strongly the nucleus attracts the outer electrons, which in turn influences electron energy levels and an atom’s chemical behavior. As a rule, a larger effective nuclear charge corresponds to a higher ionization energy, meaning more energy is needed to remove an electron, and to a stronger attraction for additional electrons.

Effective nuclear charge = (Number of protons in the nucleus) - (Number of inner-shell electrons)

The point of this formula is that inner-shell electrons block part of the nucleus’s positive charge from reaching the outer-shell electrons. For that reason, effective nuclear charge helps explain atomic reactions and the periodic trends seen across the elements."]

question: - How do lightbulbs know the correct wattage to draw from the electrical current . How does a 60 watt bulb and 20 watt bulb fit into the same socket yet know to draw more or less amps ? Explain like I'm five.

More resistance means less electrical flow , means less light , means lower wattage .", "There is a mathematical relationship between voltage , amperage , wattage and electrical resistance . To get a 60 watt bulb you have to have a bulb that draws .5454 amps on a 110 volt line ( which is what your house has for lamps ) . That is because watts = amps X volts . So you know how many amps this bulb has to draw so how do you get a bulb to draw .5454 amps ? You add resistance to the circuit ( bulb ) . To get the correct amount of resistance for a .5454 amp 60 watt bulb on a 110 volt line you need 201 ohms of resistance . We know this because resistance = volts / amps .", "Wattage is the measure of energy used per time , expressed as joules per second . Amperage is a measure of how much charge is flowing through the wire , expressed in coulombs per second . In your house , you will have many different devices that all draw different wattages and currents , but they all are made to run on the same voltage ( Joules per coulomb ) , which is 110V in the US and 240V in other places . The wattage of each device is then determined by the voltage that goes to your house and the resistance of each device , using the formula P = V^2 / R. Since all the devices run on the same voltage , they have to be made with different resistances to draw different amounts of power . For your 60 watt bulb , you can get resistance by dividing 110 ^ 2 /60 to get a resistance of 201 Ohms . Once you know the resistance , it 's easy to find the current that it draws using V = I*R ( .54 Amps ) ."]

question: Leibnizian monads How are they like building blocks of the universe ? How are they meant to be different from atoms ? Explain like I'm five.

The minds do n't perceive each other by casually affecting each other ( they are \" windowless \" ) , but rather by having all of their thoughts and perceptions sync up . So , if one monad is conscious of something , the others will have a corresponding thought or perception from a different perspective . The physical world is constructed out of the thoughts of all these little monads . Thus , Leibniz is an idealist , someone who believes that fundamental reality is mental . My best 5 year old explanation : you know how a Lego building block looks different when you look at it from different places . Now most people think the block is made of the little pieces of plastic in the block , but Leibniz thinks it 's made of all those different \" looks \" . They are not the same as atoms. Atoms are the smallest pieces of matter, and they contain protons, neutrons, and electrons. question: Memristors Have they been discovered ? What do they do ? Are they important ? Why ? What will / can change as a result of their discovery ? Explain like I'm five.

Electricity is like a waterfall . The water flows from higher points to lower points . With electricity , this we have net electric charge instead of water . Voltage is like elevation of the waterfall . Current is the amount of water that flows down the waterfall . You may have heard of Ohm 's law before . We write V = I x R , where V is voltage , I is current , and R is is something called resistance . If we have a resistor with a certain value for R , putting a specific voltage across the two terminals of the device means the same current will flow through it every time . Ohm 's law describes what this current is . Memristors are a lot like resistors . They have two terminals . Current flows through them . The difference here is that their resistance changes . The more current that flows through it , the higher the resistance goes . If you reverse the direction the current flows , the resistance gets smaller again . Now how is this useful ? We currently use something called DRAM for computer memory . Memory stores a bunch of ones and zeros . Have you ever pulled some laundry out of the dryer and got shocked ? The laundry was storing a charge and when you touched it , the charge equalized . If you touch it again , you wo n't get shocked . DRAM is like this . It stores charges on millions of devices called capacitors . If the capacitor has a charge , it stores a 1 . If it does n't , it stores a zero . Memristors can store information . If they have a high resistance , you could have that mean its storing a 1 . If it has a low resistance , you could have it mean that it is storing a zero . The big advantage of memristors over DRAM is that memristors do n't need constant power to store information . It 's like your flash drive , except way way faster .", "[ URL_0 ] ( URL_0 ) If you 're interested , you can use this simulator to poke at memristors . question: Irony , and how it differs from sarcasm . And what is the thing commonly mistaken as irony called , if not irony ? Like , if a paralyzed person wins running shoes for life . What is it called , besides tragic ? Explain like I'm five.

Sarcasm is \" a sharp , bitter , or cutting expression or remark ; a bitter jibe or taunt . \" So basically while they are differrent , they are usually found together . Other types of irony include situational irony which is the example you described of the paralyzed person winning running shoes . The last type of irony is dramatic irony and usually exists in literature . This is when the reader knows more than the character . For example in Oedipus : Oedipus is looking for the man who killed the king - the reader knows that it is him , but none of the characters do . This creates dramatic irony and usually results in * drama * .", "> And what is the thing commonly mistaken as irony called , if not irony ? Coincidence , usually . For example , if I return from lunch and I see someone leaving , and they asked where I went , I may say \" Oh , I went to Friday 's . \" If they reply \" How ironic , that 's where we 're going , \" that 's * not * irony . It 's just a coincidence . Darkfrost has some good examples . Others are like ... on LOST , Hurley wins the lottery , but then crash lands on an island where money is useless . Or in the Twilight Zone , the old dude who does n't have enough time to read , but when all of humanity is wiped out and he finally * does * have unlimited time , he breaks his reading glasses and can no longer read .", "The biggest mistake people make regarding irony is to consider that there 's only one kind of irony . For example , when you compare irony with sarcasm , you 're thinking about * verbal * irony : when you say something that means the opposite of its literal meaning . Sarcasm is very often done in the form of verbal irony . But there are many more kinds of irony that have nothing to do with sarcasm . Another example is when white knights will say \" that is not irony because irony is this [ insert link to YouTube video ] \" , often followed with a reference to Alanis Morissette . Their mistake is that they assume the person is wrong because it does n't match the one definition they know . The person is sometimes rightfully using a different form of irony . So what is irony ? At its core , it deals with an incongruity between two opposite things , but this can take several forms . * Verbal irony is when you say something and mean the opposite : \" That did n't hurt at all \" while wincing in pain . The irony is in the contrast between literal meaning and real meaning . This is the form of irony defined by [ Bender ] ( URL_1 ) . * Socratic irony is when you ask questions , feigning ignorance , to expose the weakness of an argument . The irony is in the contrast between what you know and what you pretend to know . [ Philosoraptor ] ( URL_8 ) is a master of [ socratic ] ( URL_7 ) [ irony ] ( URL_5 ) . * Dramatic irony occurs in works of fiction ( movies , books ... ) It is when the audience knows something that the character does n't . The character may unknowingly be acting towards their own demise , and the audience knows . The most poignant example is in Romeo and Juliet , Juliet drinks something that will make it look like she 's dead . She sent a message to Romeo about it but he never got it . When he finds her , he really thinks her dead and commits suicide . Juliet wakes up , realizes what happened and kills herself too . The irony is in the contrast between what the characters think will happen and what the audience know will happen instead . * Cosmic irony is when fate ( or god , or the universe ) seems to be toying with you . Like if you 're a runner but ca n't afford good shoes , then you get hit by a car and are paralized on the day you win running shoes for life . Some people see this as merely coincidental and do n't consider this to be irony . * Then there 's situational irony . It 's not the classic form of irony but it 's the most widespread nowadays , and most controversed . It 's when the outcome of a situation is the opposite of expectations , when a situation is both coincidental and contradictory in a humorous or poignant way . Like [ this ] ( URL_0 ) or [ this ] ( URL_2 ) or [ this ] ( URL_6 ) , and you 'll find people to argue that these are not examples of irony . This is the form of irony described by [ George Carlin ] ( URL_3 ) . This is the form poorly attempted by Alanis Morissette , as denounced by [ Ed Byrne ] ( URL_4 ) . There are more forms of irony , this is n't exhaustive ."]

Sarcasm is a kind of irony, but it’s usually meant to mock, tease, or criticize. People often show sarcasm through their voice or facial expression, which is why it can be hard to spot in writing.

question: Difference between monolithic and micro kernel . Which category does linux belong to ? Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["Linux is a monolithic kernel . Every kernel architect has his or her own definition of what constitutes a nano kernel , a micro kernel , a monolithic kernel , and a hybrid kernel . In general , a monolithic kernel has most , if not all of the operating system 's core elements sitting within the kernel and operating with supervisory privileges . Non - executive system services and user programs run with reduced privileges . Executive services and device drivers can communicate with one another rapidly through simple procedure calls ; however , a single poorly written component can break the whole kernel . In a hybrid kernel , some of the operating system 's core elements are removed from the kernel and run with reduced privileges alongside user programs and non - executive system services . In a microkernel , only essential resource management and communication functions sit within the kernel and run with supervisory privilege . Everything else , including executive system services , run with reduced privileges . Executive services must communicate with one through inter - process communication managed by the kernel ; this is much slower than procedure calls but a poorly written component will affect only itself .", "A monolithic kernel runs the operating in \" kernel space \" , a privileged position , and defines all the virtual interfaces over the hardware . When you write a program , you do n't tell the hard drive to open a particular file , let alone how to do it - file - open is an abstraction provided by the OS , whether it 's a hard drive , CD drive , or other device . A microkernel is very small by comparison , and handles few essential services like memory management , and has few generic interfaces which drivers and protocol stacks can interact with . Drivers and protocol stacks are n't run in privileged as the kernel itself is , they 're ran in user space , which has a different set of permissions and access control over the system , dictated by the kernel . User space has to cross logical barriers and get permission to do certain elevated tasks . Monolithic kernels have kernel space vs. user space , too , it 's just with the micro kernel , more is in user space . The advantage is anything in user space can crash or get dumped and replaced without taking down the whole system . In a monolithic kernel , if there is a bug in the file system code , it effects the whole system . Exo kernels are also an exciting branch of kernel design - these perform one role , multiplex the hardware . That 's it . Even memory management is a user space program . This allows these low level services to interact with one another without requiring kernel access ( a computationally expensive context switch ) , and each service can be custom curtailed for the application for higher performance . The Linux kernel is arguable whether it 's a micro kernel or monolithic kernel , it 's neither , not purely , at least . It does a shit ton , lots of essential services and protocols , and their interfaces , are defined by the kernel , but it can also fork that work out to modules in user space .", "An OS with a monolithic kernel has everything piled into a single \" program \" . Technically , your network stack could muck about with the data belonging to the disk access routines ( but they * should n't * ) . A microkernel OS enforces the idea that unrelated parts should n't be able to muck about with each other 's data & resources by making each part a separate \" program \" and requiring them to communicate through a small , message - passing core ( the microkernel ) so that this is the * only * way separate components can interact . All major commercial operating systems these days are monolithic . While , technically , OSX is build on top of a microkernel , it 's only running a single \" service \" that handles everything ."]
chatgpt_answers:
["A monolithic kernel is like one big bundle of software that does nearly all the work an operating system needs. It handles the hardware, runs programs, and offers services to those programs. A microkernel is much smaller: it keeps only the most basic pieces needed to keep the system alive, then depends on other programs, often called servers, to do the rest.

Linux is a monolithic kernel. That means the code that runs the operating system is all joined together in one large unit.

A simpler picture:

Think of a house. A monolithic kernel is a house where everything is in the same building — bedrooms, kitchen, bathroom, and living room all under one roof. A microkernel is more like a tiny house that only has the essentials, like walls and a roof, and it relies on separate buildings for things like the kitchen or bathroom.

Hope that helps make it clearer! If you want, I can explain it in a different way too."]

question: What ’s the difference between horsepower and torque ? So I ’ve been asked to explain this recently and I just realized I ca n’t ! What ’s a good analogy and what would one rather have more of in a high performance car ? Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["Enzo Ferrari 's famous quote is ; > Horsepower sells cars , torque wins races . Torque is basically just twisting force ; the higher the torque , the easier it is to change the rate of rotation of the output shaft ( which is connected to the wheels ) , which allows you to accelerate better with load . Ergo , the car that can put out more torque from a dead stop is the car that will accelerate faster , assuming both cars are the same weight . Horsepower is just a measure of power ( functionally no different from Watts , just bigger ) . Power is the product of torque and rotational speed , so you can achieve high horsepower either with high torque and low speeds , or with lower torque and high speeds .", "This is a very easy , down to earth explanation . Torque is the force that actually moves the car , hp is a calculation used to show how effectively you have use that torque with gearing . In * a single gear * , your cars acceleration will follow the torque curve exactly . So if you are in 1st gear , and go full throttle at 1000rpms all the way to 7000rpms , the acceleration you feel ( G forces ) will be identical to the torque curve , the car will pull the hardest at peak torque and pull the least hard at wherever the least amount of torque is . So what is hp then ? HP shows the peak efficiency of the engine with gearing . When accelerating at full throttle you normally shift around peak hp because that is the peak point of efficiency that given gear has . You can bring car speed into the equation now , at a given * car speed * you will accelerate the hardest at peak power because you will be in the lowest gear possible for that speed which multiplies the most amount of engine torque .", "This is the way I got it straight in my head so it might help you too : Torque is twisting force . Consider loosening a nut with a spanner . The spanner is 1 ft long and you push with a force of 50 lb . That 's a torque of 50ftlb ( foot pounds ) Then we look at how fast you are turning the spanner . The torque multiplied by the speed ( multiplied by a constant value which I 've forgotten for now ) will give the horsepower . The special thing to remember about the torque is that if you apply the 50ftlb of torque from earlier and the nut is too tight and does n't move , then we 're still applying torque . And as the spanner is n't moving the horsepower is 50 X 0 = 0 horsepower ."]
chatgpt_answers:
["Horsepower and torque both describe what an engine can do, but they measure different things.

Think of horsepower as how quickly the engine can get work done. It’s a lot like speed in a race: the more horsepower you have, the better the car can keep going fast over distance.

Torque is the engine’s turning force. Picture trying to twist open a jar of pickles that’s stuck really hard. The force you use to turn the lid is torque. More torque means the engine can push harder and start moving things more easily.

For a high-performance car, you usually want a lot of both. Horsepower helps it keep pulling at higher speeds, while torque is what gives it that strong punch off the line and makes it feel forceful when you step on the gas."]

human_answers: ["Different kinds of communism , known as \" tendencies \" , have different views about this but most Communists follow Marx 's thoughts on \" dialectical materialism \" . Marx did make some suggestions in terms of defining principles with which to attempt to build and change a future society : - Property is theft . In more concrete terms , communism is a movement that seeks the abolition of capitalism and class - society . Marx dedicated a significant portion of his work to describing what communism is and how it arises necessarily within capitalist society . Capitalism is , to Marx , a \" mode - of - production \" ( essentially ' the way production is organized within society ' ) that features generalised commodity - based production ( meaning that commodity - based production is predominant ) . Commodities are items that are both : use - values , or an item of utility for some people , and exchange - values , or an item that can be exchanged in a specific ratio for other commodities . For example : a chair is a use - value because people can sit in them ; a chair is an exchange - value because it can be exchanged in the market for other commodities like two pairs of shoes or one hundred dollars . How are the exchange - ratios of specific commodities determined -- for example , why does one chair = two pairs of shoes or ten dollars ? Marx says that exchange - values are an expression of value , and he draws the conclusion that the value of a commodity is determined by that commodity 's socially - necessary labour - time ( the average time it takes for society to produce it ) . He argues that value can not be created through exchange , but that the only source of value can be human labour . Exchange - values , or money , become what he terms ' capital ' : ' self - expanding value ' , or value that is creating more value through the only means to do so ( human labour ) . It is money ( M ) used to buy commodities ( C ) , including labour - power ( the worker 's capacity to labour ) and equipment that will create lead to the creation of a greater value of commodities ( C ' ) that can be exchanged for a greater amount of money ( M ' ) . M - C - M ' Marx illustrates that the existence of generalized commodity - production presupposes the existence of a class of people who do n't own capital and thus need to sell their capacity to labour in order to survive . The proletariat , or working - class , are restricted ownership of the ' means of production ' and forced to work for the class that does own them , the bourgeoisie or owning - class . Marx believes that the working - class is alienated and that their lives are ruled by and dependent on the existence of capital . He theorizes that it 's this relation to capital that makes them a ' revolutionary ' class , and that they have to emancipate themselves through an international revolution that abolishes nations , governments , money , and commodity - production worldwide . Your questions about currency are mistaken -- money does not exist in communism . Let me be clear , too : the USSR was not an example of communism or socialism . First : Marx never made a distinction between those two terms . Second : the existence of money and wage - labour in the USSR points to the fact that it was state - capitalist . The idea that it 's an example of communism or socialism is a Stalinist revision of Marxism to justify its state - capitalist nature . As far as what a communist world would look like : the means of production would be owned co - operatively by the entirety of society , there would be no government or state to enforce codified laws , people would choose to work , production would not be for the creation of surplus - value but for the needs of society , and its products would be freely distributed based on the principle : \" from each according to his ability , to each according to his needs \" . It is , in a sense , the beginning of history .", "The reality is , communism * does n't * work . The other comments give a good description of the * idea * of communism . Sure we might believe that the * idea * of communism is good , but how do we put it into practice ? That is the problem . question: Why does the earth 's rotation affect a pendulum , but not anything else that hovers above the ground ? If the earth is truly rotating , atmosphere and all , then the Foucault pendulum would n't be possible whatsoever , would it ? Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["The * Coriolis effect * happens when you 're on a rotating surface and you change your distance from the center of rotation . It 's easiest to explain if you think about standing on a merry - go - round . All the horses on the merry - go - round make one full rotation in the same amount of time . But the horses farther from the center have a longer distance to travel in that same amount of time . So they 're moving faster than the horses near the center . What if you try to jump from one horse to another ? If you 're on a horse near the center and you jump outwards , you 're jumping from a slower horse to a faster horse . As you jump outwards , you 're moving with the same speed as the slower horse was ( because of inertia ) , but suddenly you 're in a zone of faster horses . The horse you were trying to jump onto is moving faster than you are , so it outpaces you and you fall on the floor behind it . In other words , moving outwards from the center of rotation causes you to drift backwards , from the point of view of the rotating merry - go - round . The opposite happens if you jump from one of the outer horses inwards - you 're moving faster than the horse you 're aiming at , so you drift forwards . The same thing happens on Earth 's surface . The North and South Poles are at the axis of rotation , so they have no lateral speed . But the Equator is moving at over 1000 mph . So as you move from one of the Poles toward the Equator , you will drift \" backwards \" with respect to Earth 's rotation ( i.e. westward ) . If you 're moving from the Equator toward one of the Poles , you 'll drift \" forwards \" ( eastward ) . This affects any floating object to some extent . But it 's a very small effect - you only see it with things that are really really big ( like ocean currents ) or really really fast ( like long - range artillery ) .", "Well , it does . It is known as the coriolis effect . The Foucault pendulum is just a way to show that the earth is rotating by being able to measure the changes in the path of the pendulum as the earth rotates .", "It 's not so much that the earth 's rotation has an effect on the pendulum , it 's actually that it * does n't * . What 's really happening is that the pendulum keeps moving in the same direction , but the earth is rotating underneath it . There is an animation [ here ] ( URL_0 ) that shows what 's really going on ."]
chatgpt_answers:
["Earth’s spin does influence everything near the surface, even things suspended in the air. The reason you usually don’t notice it is that, in most situations, the effect is extremely tiny.

Take a falling ball as an example. Gravity makes it go down, and Earth’s rotation only bends its path a little. Because the object is small and air resistance is acting on it, that rotational effect is usually too slight to spot.

A pendulum is different. It’s long and heavy, and it keeps swinging back and forth for a long time before air resistance and other small losses slow it down much. Since it keeps moving for so long, the fact that the floor beneath it is turning with Earth becomes noticeable.

So the pendulum only seems to change direction because it is swinging relative to the ground while the ground is rotating under it. That’s why Earth’s rotation shows up clearly in a Foucault pendulum, even though the same rotation is much harder to see in most other things hovering above the ground."]

question: Why is there so much smoke produced after a fire goes out ? Even if the material seems to burn clean before it goes out . Explain like I'm five.

Details : What is smoke ? It 's soot , and carbon compounds , tar , aerosols , un burned fuel , and other components of incomplete combustion . When a fire burns brightly and hotly , it efficiently combines oxygen with carbon to make carbon dioxide and carbon monoxide , and with hydrogen to make water vapor . If all of the hydrocarbons and organic material being burned are done so cleanly , then there is very little smoke and instead an almost pure reaction resulting in carbon dioxide and water which are odorless and transparent gases . IE not smoke . When a flame is extinguished but there is still a lot of latent heat , the process of burning is snuffed out resulting in incomplete transformation into the water and carbon dioxide , leading to incomplete compounds which create the soot .", "Smoke is fine particulate matter suspended in a gaseous stream . Air currents can stir up these fine particles just as easily as outgassing from a fire . Every drop of water stirs it up . Fire requires 3 pieces to exist : heat , oxygen , & fuel . Water reduces the heat of the fire . Halogen removes the oxygen . The fire may be reduced in its ability to reignite but the fine particles of ash already produced can still become airborne , hence smoke long after the fire is out .", "I do n't know that this expands to other fires , but candles seem to only produce smoke when they 've just been put out because that \" smoke \" is actually evaporating wax that is most of the fuel . question: Why are propellers on the back of boats , but the front of airplanes ? Never made sense to me . Seems like airplanes play by their own set of rules Please explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["For reasons of balance , it 's much easier to have the engine at the front of a plane than at the back - unless you 're flying a canard , which has the wings at the back and the \" tailplane \" at the front . Boats , on the other hand , have a lot less problems with balance , and it 's easier to put the engine amidships or towards the back . Add to that water being more viscous so you do n't want high - speed flow dragging all along the hull , it makes sense to put the prop at the back .", "Lots of reasons- If you put the propeller at the back of the plane- then when you rotate on takeoff or flare for landing- you put the propeller dangerously close to the ground . Airflow over the fuselage and wings gets pretty turbulent and when that air interacts with the propeller the propeller becomes noisier and less efficient . Waterflow over a boat hull tends to be a lot less turbulent and the propellers are either spinning much slower ( large boat ) or located below the hull where there is even less turbulence ( smaller boats ) . Cooling an air cooled engine can be difficult when the fuselage is in the way . Putting the engine in front greatly simplifies cooling baffles and such . question: How do tattoo last so long if every cell in your body is replaced in 7 years ? I am aware that they fade , has that go to do with cells recycling ? Please explain like I'm five.
