Anything drawn onto the skin would gradually flake or get washed off . But tattoos are done by using machines to punch through the outer layer of skin and into the dermis , which is the deeper layer of skin that does n’t flake off . They do that by pushing small needles over and over , quickly , pushing the ink deep into the fibers of the dermis . Pushing so far into the skin is essentially a wound , and the body reacts as you ’d expect it to . The immune system reacts to what it considers to be an attack by sending cells to the wound and start repairing the skin — that process might sound traumatic , but it ’s actually what allows tattoos to stick around for so long . The cells , known as macrophages , travel to the site of the wound and start eating up the ink that has been deposited there . Other cells and parts of the skin , such as fibroblasts , suck up the tattoo ink . But they ca n’t do anything with it , and so stay stuck in that layer , visible through the skin . Some of the ink will go into the upper layer of the skin , and that will then be shed as normal . That ’s why the process of getting a tattoo also includes time letting your skin heal , with the body taking two to four weeks to initially heal . question: What happens when a power company generates too much power ? When a power generation station produces too much power what do they do with the excess ? Who pays for , what I 'm assuming , is wasted output ? Please explain like I'm five.

question: How do " hiveminds " of ant colonies actually work ? Together , ants seem to be capable of highly coordinated efforts between thousands of ants . It 's just amazing to me . How does that coordination actually work ? Please explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["A hive mind is n't a single mind in the way sci fi shows make you think . It would be more accurate to think of it in terms of a great many individuals , each behaving in such a way that would be fairly stupid for that individual ... but when everyone does it , it becomes pretty smart . Let me give you an example . When ants forage for food , they just wander around in a mostly random manner , hoping to stumble across a food source . * Let 's say ant A is foraging for food , wandering randomly until it finds a discarded muffin . * Ant A grabs a muffin crumb and heads back to the colony , at the same time it puts down a scent trail from the muffin to the colony . * Ant B is also foraging randomly but it finds the scent trail laid down by ant A. Ant B has a very simple decision to make . Either follow the scent trail to see if something 's at the source of it or continue to forage randomly . * Let 's say it 's a 50/50 decision for ant B and for the sake of argument , ant B decides to follow the scent trail . * Ant B finds the muffin , grabs a crum , heads back to the nest and lays down his own scent trail , reinforcing the trail laid down by ant A. * Ant C is foraging randomly and also finds the scent trail but by now it 's been reinforced by ant B. The much stronger scent trail makes it more likely that ant C will follow the trail instead of continuing to forage randomly . * Ant C decides to follow the trail , grabs a crumb and heads back to the colony . Also reinforcing the scent trail . * For foraging ant D it 's even more likely that he 'll follow the trail instead of continuing his random search pattern after ant C reinforced the trail . * and so on , and so on . Now each of these ants is behaving in exactly the same way . They all forage randomly until they find either food or a scent trail and then make a decision on whether the continue their random search or to follow the scent trail . The primary variable is that the stronger the scent trail is , the more likely any new ants that find the trail are the follow it . In practical terms this means that a small food source will attract very few ants . The scent trail will be very weak because the food source will be gone before many ants are attracted . Any ants that follow the weak scent trail and find no food , wo n't reinforce the trail and just go back to searching randomly . Without frequent reinforcement , the trail will just dissipate fairly quickly . A large food source on the other hand will keep attracting more and more ants as the scent trail keeps getting stronger and more persuasive to fresh ants that find the trail . Individually all those ants are behaving in the same , not so intelligent way . They 're not even directly communicating to each other , they just have instinctive responses to finding food or scent trails . But the net result is that the ant colony as a whole efficiently assigns the appropriate number of ants to deal with large and small food sources . Large food sources attract many ants . Small food sources will never waste the time of many ants . That 's essentially what a hive mind is . Many individuals displaying simple behaviour that compounds into complex results when many individuals behave that way together .", "Their minds are not actually linked , but they all share similar instincts which have been developed over thousands of years . These instincts tell them how to act as a hive and follow each other . Colonies of animals also use pheromones which are chemical messages that other animals can detect . These can warn the colony of danger , food , etc . The culmination of instinct and pheromones make it look like the entire colony share one mind .", "Not really a hive mind in the sci - fi sense . Each ant just has the genetically coded instinct to perform a certain role , such as protecting from invaders , collecting food , or in the Queen 's case , reproduction . They use pheromone trains to communicate sources of food , danger , etc"]
chatgpt_answers: ["Ants keep in sync using chemicals and very simple actions. In a colony, every ant has a job, and they pass along information with chemical signals called pheromones. For instance, if one ant discovers food, it leaves behind a pheromone trail that other ants can follow. Once more ants notice that scent, they’re more likely to move in the same direction, which helps the colony find the food and carry it back.\n\nThey also communicate by touching antennae, and they use basic rules like matching the ants around them or joining the biggest group. Put together, these little tricks let thousands of ants act as a coordinated team, even without a boss or any complicated language."]

question: What exactly happens when I click on " Safely Eject " for a USB or external hard drive ? . Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["When you save a file to disk or to a USB stick , it usually is not written completely at the moment of saving . Writing to disk means that your file is written to a part of your computer 's memory , after which the operating system tells your program the file has been saved . In the background , the data of your file is then transferred from this * memory buffer * to the storage medium ( that is : your disk or USB stick ) . So after you have saved a file , parts of it remain in memory and have not yet been written out to the storage medium . However , your program has told * you * that the file was successfully stored . When you prepare a USB stick or external hard disk for ejection , you tell your computer to finish writing out all data that is currently lingering in the memory buffer , while at the same time rejecting any new files to be written to the device . After the buffer has been * flushed * ( that is what it 's called ) all parts of all your file(s ) are actually stored on the hard disk or USB stick and it is safe to disconnect it from your computer . The real - life situation is a bit more complicated , because in addition to writing the file 's content to the storage medium , it also takes some housekeeping reads and writes to register * where * exactly your data was stored in the file system of the external drive or USB stick . tl;dr : pending data needs to be written out to the storage medium from the memory buffer . Edit : removed some * actually*s and polished up lightly .", "No one knows , it has never been done", "The reason this \" safely remove \" option exists is just as a sort of double - check that you 're not removing the drive in the middle of a file transfer . Most external drives now have an activity light that blinks when the drive is in use , and you 're fine just going by that . As long as it 's not blinking , the drive can be removed at any time ."]

Here’s the idea:

Your computer stops communicating with the drive: After you choose “Safely Eject,” it stops reading from and writing to that device. So if a file was open or being changed, the computer finishes it up, saves it, and closes it.

Edit : spelling .", "So this is in the context of cancer research and by a non - PhD ( so I basically understand this LI5 ): If we take a chunk of tissue from a person or animal , we can put that tissue on a DNA Microarray and run it through a very large , very expensive machine and it will tell us what genes are present in that tissue - and in what quantities . Instead of growing and acting normally , the cells in your body have decided to grow unrestrained and start to take over the normal , healthy cells . Cancer is not like a cold or polio where there is a single virus or bacteria that caused it . It 's caused by your body mutating itself . So the big question is - why ? Why did the cells in your body decide to start mutating ? That 's what we use microarrays to help figure out . By putting a tumor sample on a microarray , we measure what genes are present in the tumor and in what quantities . The hope is that we can compare there results of the tumor measurement to that from a sample of normal tissue and determine which genes are possibly contributing to the mutation . For example , we might see that in a lung cancer patient that gene XXXX should have a level of 2 , but in their tumor has a level of 10 . There 's some variations of this , but this is the basis of microarrays . question: How do apps like shazam and soundhound work ? Once they listen to the song , how do they find out what song it is ? Do they find like a matching audio file or something like that ? Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["I do n't know about Soundhound , but for Shazam specifically , here is how it works ( or at least did a few years ago when I read about this ) ; As the Shazam app records the song , it will split it up into short blocks of time . Within each period of time , it will figure out what frequency is the loudest . Assuming that the music is the loudest thing happening , this does an excellent job of filtering out any background noise and quiet notes that may be imperceptible to a crappy cell phone microphone . It does n't matter if people are talking or a door opens or if your cell mic ca n't pick out that exquisite bass guitar in the background -- only the loudest thing matters . The result is a \" fingerprint \" of the song --- a list of the loudest frequencies versus time for a short fragment . The frequencies are rounded off here , because 14224 kHz is not going to be distinguishable from 14000 kHz for the purposes of Shazam and your crappy cell mic . Because you obviously do n't record the entire song , the actual fingerprint you get on your phone will be a subset of the complete fingerprint of the song . Like this ( random letters used to represent frequencies ) Full song : ABAJUKARWA Clip : UKA ( Note that , at this point , Shazam does n't know that the clip and the full song are associated , much less where in the full song the clip appears , but they are presented together for demonstration ) . Note that the fingerprint from your mic is actually pretty close to the real one , even though you just held your crappy cell phone towards the mall speaker for ten seconds . That is for a few reasons : 1 . As previously mentioned , only the loudest sounds are used 2 . Because you 're not listening to radio static , the song at 59.2 seconds sounds really similar to the song at 59.3 seconds . Therefore the impact of your time intervals not necessarily being aligned with the \" official \" time intervals is reduced . Now , Shazam has invested a lot of money into buying studio music and fingerprinting entire albums . So they have an enormous database of songs and their associated complete fingerprints ( what the loudest sounds are in each small time fragment for the entire song ) . Your phone uploads the fingerprint it took to the Shazam server . Shazam uses a proprietary fuzzy matching algorithm to very quickly search their huge database of fingerprints for portions that are very close to your fingerprint . They do n't use an exact match ; they actually use a line similarity algorithm . Basically , imagine the full song as an XY graph with time as X and loudest frequency in that time fragment as Y. Your clip is a small part of that graph . Under studio conditions the lines would be exactly identical . On a crappy cell mic , they wo n't be , but : 1 . They will be quite close ( if the song 's loudest frequency is 20kHz at some point , your mic might might be a frequency band or so up or down , but it 's not going to be 7kHz ) 2 . They will have the same patterns of rise and fall , 3 . If there are deviations due to loud noises , they will be short ( maybe someone talked or a door opened , but that 's just a big blip and then it goes back to normal ) . Using these three facts , Shazam is able to do a really , really good job of identifying what song your clip came from , and where . And then it sends back the result ! :)", "It 's like a fingerprint . Shazam has a huge library of songs for which it has already analyzed the frequencies and created a \" digital fingerprint , \" like a [ spectrogram ] ( URL_0 ) . When you use it to identify a song , it performs this same analysis on the music it 's \" listening \" to and compares the results to its library to find the song that matches . Like a human fingerprint , a particular song will have a unique pattern of frequencies , which is why it usually only needs to \" hear \" a small clip in order to narrow it down enough find the matching song .", "Most likely it analyzes the piece of sound waves you make it listen to , and compares that real time with a database full of songs and sound waves . I 'm no expert in this field , but there are tons of algorithms designed to understand music better , so I 'm sure Shazam and Soundhound got a hold of those algorithms ."]
chatgpt_answers:
["Shazam and SoundHound use your phone’s microphone to pick up the music that’s playing, then compare what they hear against a huge library of songs. They’re looking for a match based on each song’s special sound features, like melody, rhythm, and instrumentation.

Here’s the idea: when you tap the app’s “listen” button, it records the sound coming through the microphone. After that, it turns that recording into a digital “fingerprint,” which is a small piece of data that summarizes the song’s sound. That fingerprint gets sent to a server, where it’s checked against the database of song fingerprints to see whether anything matches. If the server finds a match, it sends the song information back to the app, and the app shows you the title and artist.

So it’s not really hunting for a whole audio file that’s identical. It’s comparing the fingerprint of the sound it heard with fingerprints already saved in the database to figure out what song it is."]

question: Why are things lighter under water ? I remember being a kid and picking up heavy rocks under water at the lake . Why is weight different under water ? Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["Buoyancy , which is caused by differences in its density due to pressure due to gravity . Basically , when you place an object in a liquid / gas ( water ) , it gets pushed back up due to how much volume it displaces . The amount of force pushing the object up is proportional to the density of the liquid / gas and the volume the object displaces . This makes heavier objects in water feel lighter than they are in air . This also makes boats float . This actually also occurs in air- but because air is nt nearly as dense as water you do nt notice it unless you have an helium balloon .", "Buoyancy . Basically , buoyancy is an upward force experienced on a object when it is immersed in a fluid ( liquid or gas ) . It works because pressure increases in a fluid the lower you go , meaning the pressure on the bottom of the object is greater than the pressure at the top , it creates a force that pushes the object up . Because force is an additive quantity , the upward force cancels some of the downward force ( the weight ) .", "Technically an object under water is no lighter than it is out of water . Weight is a result of the gravitational pull on an object . When in the water , a certain amount of bouyancy offsets the weight , caused by a difference of densities between the water and the object . Out of water there is no bouyancy , so the object has no resistance to gravity , making it seem heavier ."]

question: What is the difference between computer hardware and smartphone hardware ? Extended question : What is preventing people from developing proper drivers and running iOS Android devices and vise versa like you can with osx and windows ? Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["The main difference is that most smartphones have ARM processors , whereas PC 's have either Intel or AMD . More specifically , smartphones use a RISC architecture , whereas PC 's use CISC . RISC stands for \" reduced instruction set computing . \" What that means is when an instruction ( command ) gets passed to the processor , it does exactly one thing . So , if I wanted to add two numbers together , the assembly code might look something like : lw $ t0 , 0($s0 ) //load the first number from memory lw $ t1 , 4($s0 ) //load the second number from memory add $ t2 , $ t0 , $ t1 //add the two numbers sw $ t3 , 8($s0 ) //store the result in memory In other words , I 'm doing four steps to perform one operation ( addition ) . In a CISC ( complex instruction set computing ) architecture , instructions can do more than one task at a time . I do n't know the actual assembly for it , but I could write the stuff up there in one line in CISC . The key difference is that the CISC instruction can do the load and store commands in the same instruction as the addition . The advantage of CISC is that you can perform more advanced tasks with fewer instructions , but the disadvantage is that the increased level of complexity means you need more power and resources . RISC is \" lean \" and efficient , but not quite as flexible . None of this necessarily has anything to do with why you ca n't put iOS on your phone . If I had to hazard a guess , I would say that it could be done , but nobody has enough motivation ( financial or otherwise ) to actually do it .", "People can and do . I used to run Android on my iPhone 3 G , but the drivers were not so good , especially for power management , which meant that it would empty a full battery in about 40 minutes . This is a licensing and marketing issue , not a technical one . Originally , PCs had to make it very easy for people to load whatever OS they wanted , because there was no clearly dominant OS and people who were into early computers were enthusiasts who liked to tinker . Today , Apple has a vested interest in convincing people to switch from Windows , so they go out of their way to make it possible for people to load both OSs on their hardware , so that people can get a migration path - a way to try out Mac OS without fear of losing Windows forever . The PC architecture still descends from the original PC , which made it easy to load different OSs . Microsoft is working hard to change this , but for now they need to keep compatible , and also there are many niche applications that rely on OSs other than Windows in the PC arena , such as Linux ( and other Unix variants ) on servers , specialized computers for scientific instruments , and more . On the smartphone side , on the other hand , Apple started by itself . They have no need and no incentive to be compatible with anything else , and they try to protect their market by making it as hard as possible to install anything other than iOS on an iPhone . More or less the same goes for the other players , who came later to this market . There is some basic computer science that shows that they can make it difficult , but never impossible , to run any software on any hardware . Finally , there 's a matter of licensing and legality . Just because it 's technically possible , it does n't mean it 's legal .", "Size basically . Also the stuff in smartphones tends to be optimized to use less power and generate less heat . Also licensing is what prevents it , Apple does n't allow you to do such a thing and releases no information to help anybody do it against the licensing . If Apple wanted they could release an Android IPhone or IOS Nexus device if they partnered with Google ."]

question: Why is it that calling / texting another country costs more but when visiting a website that s hosted in another country does n't effect price . Surely they use similar cable runs ( not share ) so where s the cost incurred ? Explain like I'm five.

The telephone system is separate from the internet , and uses different cables . The big difference is that while the internet uses packages , the phone system uses a dedicated channel , which gets set up when you place the call , and stays in place while the call is active . The cable between the different countries can only handle a certain number of channels , so they charge for usage of them , to prevent them from getting flooded . VOIP works for the most part , but has to deal with bigger lag ( as each package gets routed separately ) , dropped packages , and the possibility of packages arriving out of order ( if they took different routes ) . Essentially , back in the day phone lines where actual copper all the way . Copper run from your house to your local exchange , and from there nationwide . International calls where part copper , part radio , radio being either land - to - land or land - satellite - land . The entire network did n't belong to the same operator , and even if it did , he still had increased costs for a longer call . Satellite bandwidth is insanely expensive . Nowdays , it 's all IP . Even companies that sell you regular landlines have their backbones in IP , and their connection to other carriers is over IP too . It 's fiber all the way , and a phone call uses relatively low bandwidth , so the physical cost of actually calling someone on the other side of the globe is negligible , most of their cost is in the last mile and backbone . But of course , companies are still milking a thing of the past , so , when you want to buy minutes from another exchange , that exchange will sell you minutes at a different price than it sells them locally , even if that provider is the local exchange for that particular destination . So , say , the phone company in Somewhere , Some country will sell minutes at 0.01 to their local users in Somewhere , or will even offer unlimited plans , but when Someotherplace , Someothercountry wants to interconnect with them , Somewhere will sell ( and buy ) those same minutes at a higher price , when actually their cost of selling them to Someotherplace is lower , since it 's a telco to telco interconnection , which most of the time is just a SIP account , and the maintenance and setup costs of that trunk is lower than a single last mile for a single local customer ."]

question: How are engineers able to build subway tunnels under already built infrastructure ? It amazes me when I 'm in the subway in big cities how these tunnels have been dug beneath skyscapers ? How do the builders do it ? Please explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["There are multiple ways that they can do it . Here in Boston where I live they finished the [ Big Dig ] ( URL_2 ) a few years ago . Over the course of roughly ten years they consolidated all the utilities through a corridor of the city and then sank an entire elevated highway underground . It had to go above and below various obstacles like a subway tunnel , a waterway , and Boston Harbor itself . The main corridor of the sunken highway , I-93 , was built in multiple sections . In parts of it they simply closed down the ground - level street for a few months , dug a 100 foot deep pit , built the tunnel , then filled the hole back in . In another part where they had to dig directly under the existing elevated highway they first sunk steel girders 100 feet down to the bedrock , filled the area around them with concrete , then supported the existing highway on top of that while digging out the dirt and building the tunnel underneath . At one spot they had to build the tunnel underneath an existing subway station that was itself underneath a very busy surface road in the middle of downtown Boston . To do this they first dug two vertical shafts to below the level of the subway station . They then dug horizontal tunnels underneath the station , supporting them with concrete . From those tunnels they dug hundreds of tiny shafts and filled them up with concrete . Eventually they formed it all into a complete set of concrete walls that formed the new tunnel . If you scroll close to [ the bottom of this page ] ( URL_1 ) you can see a series of graphics that show how it was done . Another section of tunnel had to be built under multiple train tracks just outside one of Boston 's main train stations where thousands of commuters pass every day . The soil under the train tracks was pretty soft so they had to figure out a way of building this tunnel without making the tracks sink into a sinkhole or anything like that . What they did in this case was to sink hundreds of pipes into the ground and then injected freezing water into them in order to freeze the ground solid . They then dug a pit on one side of the tracks and built an entire tunnel section there . They then used giant hydraulic jacks to pull the tunnel section underneath the tracks as they slowly dug out the frozen dirt underneath them . [ Here 's a page ] ( URL_0 ) that describes that process . Although not used in Boston , another typical way of building tunnels is by using a tunnel boring machine ( TBM ) . A TBM is basically a crawling machine that can dig out dirt or rock in front of it and then immediately seal the circular hole it creates with reinforced concrete .", "Very , very carefully . They 'd normally avoid it , and subway tunnels go under roads : but if they dig deep enough to avoid weakening the foundations of a building , it can be done , assuming the local geology will allow . Sometimes buildings may be built with the expectation that a subway tunnel will be built under it in the near future , and the foundations designed accordingly . It does n't always go well , though . In 2009 , construction of a subway line near the Historcal Archive of the City of Cologne , Germany , caused the ancient building and two other buildings to collapse , mercifully causing only two deaths . However , 90 % of the archive , including documents going back to the year 922 , was buried . About 85 % of them have been recovered , but some of them badly damaged . A new building is due for completion in 2017 , but restoring the documents is likely to take another 30 years . The tunnel was n't even under the building , but next to it , and being built using the traditional \" cut and cover \" method . But water seeped out of the tunnel and into the buildings ' foundations ; workers were able to raise the alarm in time , hence the very few casualties , but only just .", "With London if I remember correctly there had n't been good documentation of buildings to account for depth of all that the tube would pass under . Some lines like the Piccadilly line and Northern line are incredible deep to account for this . Others building surveryors were called in to assess already built buildings to judge whether they could withstand a tube line being built and at what depth ."]

Where we start getting different is in the next level of structure : Our cells clump together and start working together to produce tissues , which make up organs , which make whole animals ; Yeast do n't do that . Before I can get to the subject of your question , I need to cover some background info . All living organisms are comprised of cells . Cells are rather complicated , but fundamentally are a drop of water(with alot of stuff dissolved in it ) , shielded from the world inside an oil droplet . This oil makes up the ' cell membrane ' and it gives the cell a distinct boundary , and helps the cell hold onto ' what it owns ' or what 's inside that oil wall . Cells need to ' hold onto ' a certain amount of nutrients , and various molecules otherwise they die ; if they lose their cell membrane , they die . That extremely thin wall of oil is of the utmost importance to a cell 's survival . Alcohol , kills things by dissolving their cell membrane , and releasing everything that used to be inside the cell . Normally Oil and water do n't mix , but alcohols serve as middle men , and allows them to get along just fine . There is some cool chemistry involved in that , but it 's a bit past ELI5 I think . Yeast does n't normally make ethanol actually , it does that as a back up plan . Left alone under normal conditions , yeast actually likes to make carbon dioxide using oxygen . This keeps going until the oxygen is depleted . Now , at this point the yeast has two options : produce no energy and Die , or start generating energy in a way that does n't require oxygen . Yeast decide to do the second , but this is a ' hail mary play ' as it were because the byproduct of this avenue is ethanol , which once enough of it is produced , will kill the yeast as discussed above . It 's a gamble that oxygen will come back before the yeast makes enough ethanol to kill itself . In wine making , and industry processes , the yeast always dies because we care about the ethanol , not the yeast . question: Where does fat go when you are losing weight by strictly dieting I lost 50lbs in 3months by undergoing a calorie deficit , but I never knew how fat would leave my body , through digestive means , sweat , etc . Please explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["Okay I think most of the people on here are missing the point . To be fair I am not a biologist but the way you loose weight in an overly simplistic sense is by exhaling . You sweat and poop out water and things you ate but did not absorb respectively . When you inhale you breath in oxygen O2 when you exhale you breathe out CO2 and H2O. The process by which you create energy is roughly the same as burning sugar with a bunch of complicated chemistry in between ( Krebs cycle etc . for the more interested ) . So when you have a calorie deficit your body goes into its fat stores and converts the energy stored in the fats to energy your body can use and you breath out some of the weight . That is why calories burned can be measured most accurately by measuring how much oxygen you breath in - how much you breath out it tells them how much was used to \" burn off calories \" . Real experts feel free to correct anything I screwed up", "Your body consumes the fat as fuel , to produce energy .", "Breathing , sweating , through oils that are washed off by showering or sweating , peeing , pooping , by producing skin and the skin cells dying and falling off , and hair / nail production and cutting , to name a few . The mass balance of a human system is pretty complex ."]
chatgpt_answers:
["When you lose weight, your body is basically burning its own stored fat for fuel. That fat gets broken down into smaller pieces called fatty acids, and your cells use those for energy. The name for this fat-breaking process is lipolysis.

Lipolysis happens in fat cells, which are where the body keeps its fat reserves. If you’re not eating enough calories, your body sends a message that tells those cells to let go of some of that stored fat. Once it’s released, it’s split into fatty acids and moved through the bloodstream.

From there, your body’s cells, including muscle cells, can use those fatty acids as energy. That’s part of why exercise can help with weight loss: it helps your cells make use of the fatty acids being released.

So the short answer is that fat doesn’t mostly leave through sweat or digestion. It’s broken down by lipolysis, released from fat cells into the blood, and then used by your body as energy."]

question: Why do software sometimes crashes ? Why a piece of software stop working ? Is it because it 's trying to divide per zero , for example ? Why do not programmers fully test software before selling it to you ? Please explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["Programmer here . There are * lots * of reasons why a piece of software may crash . A divide - by - zero error is one possible reason , though not the most common . Running out of memory -- or accessing the wrong section of memory -- is another potential cause . Another possibility is running out of the bounds of an array -- for example , if the program 's code says to \" look up the first five items \" , and there are only four items . The number of potential reasons for a program to crash are as numerous as programs themselves . Why is n't the software fully tested ? Well ... we try , as a rule . But the number of use - cases ( i.e. , \" What can the user do ? \" ) for complex software can make this difficult , especially if the business department is pushing for the software to be released soon ( buggy software can be patched , and unreleased software earns no money . ) To look at a relatively simple program , consider a calculator . Adding two positive numbers together is a use case . Adding a negative and a positive is a different use case . So is adding an integer to a non - integer . So is multiplying two positive integers . Every possible combination of operators and types of numbers is a different use case . And that 's just a calculator . After a certain point , it becomes difficult to even remember all the possible use cases , let alone test for all of them . Most programmers I know will try to test as they go along , but even then , and even with separate QA departments ( which you 'll find at most larger companies ) , it 's possible for certain things to slip through . And then there are the problems that arise from outside one 's own code . Software does n't exist in a vacuum . If I write a program , it has to run on your machine , with that machine 's hardware and operating system , and the drivers that are installed on it .... And all of * those * things have their own bugs . Even if the code I wrote were theoretically perfect , it could crash due to something being wrong with one of those . If my company has a large QA department , they can cover a lot of different combinations of OSs , drivers , hardware , and so forth ... but they can never cover every combination , at least not with computers ( consoles , having a uniform configuration , were a different matter , though nowadays that 's changed as well due to being able to upgrade system software . ) If you 've ever noticed with some games that some people have a consistent crash problem and other players do n't , this is often the reason why : it 's a bug that comes about through the interaction with a specific combination of hardware and software , and not all users have that combination . And finally , there are cases that are simple user error . Sometimes this can be accounted for by the programmer ; I can prevent you from entering the word \" blue \" when I 'm asking for a number . But a programmer ca n't prevent you from , for example , running more programs than your system can handle . If someone attempted to run three separate 3D renderers on a 4 GB memory system and then started up * Batman : Arkham City * , it would n't matter how good a job the programmers did at making all those programs operate efficiently , they 're going to bring that system to a screeching halt .", "The reasons for program crashes are vast , in practice there are several that cover almost all cases : Bad pointer : Trying to dereference a piece of memory that has not been allocated , or previously allocated but later deallocated with your program not acknowledging it . Unhandled Exception : Your divide by zero is one example of an exception in IEEE 754 . An exception is not strictly an unrecoverable error , but rather an extreme condition that could potentially be reasonable handled by default but is often better yielded to the program . If such an exception is thrown and the program does not have a handling routine for it the program halts due to lacking a determine execution path . Unresponsive : APIs such as Windows and DirectX expect a program to regularly call on these , such as to retrieve new input data . If a program has a long running process in the same thread as these contexts are created without regularly pausing to acknowledge the APIs , the program will fail to update inputs , window , etc . The program might be able to recover once this long running process is finished , but quite often the OS marks it as unresponsive , as does the user . Such continuous processes should either be chunked ( not run for longer than milliseconds at a time ) , or ran in a separate thread . Buffer overflow : For example allocating 4 bytes for some user input ( name ) [ M][A][R][K ] , having the user input a longer name and attempting to write this to memory without checking length [ F][R][A][N ] K. This is related to bad pointers in the sense that an attempt is being made to read or write a piece of memory that the program has not requested beforehand . This does n't necessarily lead to conventional overflow crashes however . If the memory being overflowed to has been allocated you will be stomping on some other piece of your data without the OS being aware . This could result in all sorts of bizarre behaviors . Stack overflow : When a function executes it creates a local context in some preallocated piece of memory . When one function calls another and that one calls another without the prior function terminating ( this is often seen in recursive algorithms ) , if this tree grows sufficiently large it will no longer be able to fit into the stack . Logical error : In this case the program is n't distinctly misbehaving with respect to the OS or memory , however it contains some flawed piece of logical that leads to crash - like effects . The program might for one reason or another decide that window updates are no longer necessary effectively freezing the program visually .", "> Why do not programmers fully test software before selling it to you ? Because it 's effectively impossible . One huge problem is that you are developing for a system that is n't testable . So , I might make a program on my computer but there is something in your configuration that messes things up ."]
chatgpt_answers: ["Software can stop working, or crash, for lots of different reasons. Sometimes the program is asked to do something that can’t be done, like divide by zero, which is a math operation that isn’t allowed. Other times it may run out of memory, use too much processing power, or run into a bug in the code that makes it act in a way the programmer didn’t expect. Programmers do test software carefully before selling it, but they can’t always catch every issue. Software can be extremely complicated, and it’s hard to know exactly how it will behave in every possible situation. On top of that, people often use software in ways the creators never planned, which can bring up new problems. Even so, programmers keep trying to make their software as dependable and stable as they can."]

question: Russell 's Paradox This is a topic that we were discussing today briefly in math . I 'm having trouble visualizing it . Can someone help me out ? Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["Russell 's paradox is : Let R be the set of all sets that do not contain themselves . Is R in R ? If R is in R , then R contains itself , so by the definition of R , R is not in R. If R is not in R , then R does not contain itself , so by the definition of R , R is in R. As an analogy , let 's think about a town full of people instead of sets . R is going to be a barber . And instead of talking about containing , we 'll talk about shaving . Russell 's paradox becomes : There 's a town with a barber . In this town , the barber shaves everyone who does not shave themselves . Who shaves the barber ? If the barber does not shave himself , then the barber shaves himself ( because the barber shaves * everyone * who does not shave themselves ) . If the barber does shave himself , then the barber does not shave himself ( because the barber does n't shave anyone who does shave themselves ) .", "It 's a way to say with sets , \" This statement is a lie . \"", "Suppose you 're going through your computer madly organizing files into folders and folders into other folders into other folders and so on . You 're creating the ultimate organization system . You have a \" movies \" folder and then a \" folders which contain folders which contain movies \" folder , which of course contains the \" movies \" folder , and a \" folder which contains folders which contain folders which contain entertainment \" foder , which of course contains the \" folders which contain folders which contain movies \" folder . And so on . You 're crazy . So at one point it occurs to you that your computer is letting you put folders * inside of themselves * . That is , you 're able to store the \" movies \" folder in the \" movies \" folder . You open up the movies folder and it says like - Spice World ( 1997 HD rip ) - Porn Folder - Movies Folder And if you click \" Movies Folder \" , it opens up the exact same folder . So you decide , in order to be truly organized , you ought to make a folder called the \" Folders which contain themselves \" folder . So , Movies Folder goes in there , since you just put Movies Folder in Movies Folder a moment ago . But \" Documents \" , which only contains a short story you started working on a little while ago , does not go in \" Folders which contain themselves \" . Now for the sake of completeness , you create another folder called \" Folders which do not contain themselves \" . You think that this way you can just have these two folders on your desktop — since * every * folder ought to fit in one of those , right ? After all , a folder either does or does not contain itself . If it does it goes in the first folder , and if it does n't it goes in the second folder . But I ask you : does \" Folders which do not contain themselves \" contain itself ? Well suppose it does . Then it is a folder which contains itself , so it certainly does not belong in \" Folders which do not contain themselves \" . So it must not contain itself , right ? Well if it does n't , then it is a folder which does not contain itself , so it * does * belong in \" Folders which do not contain themselves \" . So it does if it does n't and it does n't if it does . That 's a problem ."]
chatgpt_answers:
["Sure! Russell’s paradox shows up when we try to make “the set of all sets that do not contain themselves.”

Picture a set A that contains every set that is not a member of itself. So if some set B is in A, that means B does not include itself.

The problem is asking about B itself: does B contain itself or not?

- If B does contain itself, then it cannot belong to A, since A only has sets that do not contain themselves. But if B is not in A, then it would be a set that does not contain itself, which means it should be in A. Contradiction.
- If B does not contain itself, then it fits the rule for A and should be in A. But once it is in A, it has to be a set that does not contain itself. That also leads to a contradiction.

No matter which answer you pick, you run into a problem. That loop is the paradox.

Bertrand Russell found this in 1901, and it shook up mathematics because it forced people to rethink the foundations of set theory and math itself."]

question: How can something sound like it 's going up in pitch , but really it 's not ? ( Example song inside ) From the best game ever : URL_0 But really , how does that work ? Explain like I'm five.
human_answers: ["That is referred to as a [ Shepard Scale ] ( URL_0 ) . Essentially , several scales are played at once with the pitch of all of them rising in sync , but with the volume of each fading away at the high pitches , and a new \" voice \" fading in at the low pitches .", "This video by Vi Hart does an awesome job explaining some of the principles behind pitches : URL_0 What I think they 're doing in that music is they 're playing with overtones . Vi Hart demonstrates an interesting trick . If you hear ( say ) sound at 440 Hz , 660 Hz , 880 Hz , and 1100 Hz , it sounds like it 's at 220 , because your brain knows that a 220 Hz pitch is the most likely explanation for why you 're hearing all those overtones . So here 's an aural illusion for you . Suppose I play tones at 220 , 440 , 660 , 880 , 1100 , and 1320 at volumes 0 , 10 , 8 , 6 , 4 , and 2 , respectively . At this moment the 220 tone is totally muted , but it sounds like the pitch is at 220 . Now suppose over the next 5 seconds , I smoothly double each frequency , and smoothly diminish the volumes from 10 to 8 , from 8 to 6 , etc , but smoothly increase the volume of the lowest tone from 0 to 10 . From moment to moment , it sounds like the sound is going up : about 1/3 of a second in , the tones would be 230 , 460 , 690 , 920 , 1150 , and 1380 , which will sound like a 230 Hz pitch , a little higher than the 220 we started with . But after the full 5 seconds , we 're exactly back where we started . The 220 at 0 volume has become a 440 at volume 10 , the 440 at volume 10 has become 660 at volume 8 , and so on . I think this is what 's happening in that song , but they 're covering it up by having the music move quickly .", "The official name for this phenomenon / technique is The Shepard Tone . When you know what you 're listening to , you can hear it ' start over ' . For example , while watching the video you linked to , I could hear the bass come back in , and shortly after , the higher notes faded away . Seeing as though the scale is never actually ' resolved ' , our brains are always waiting for the end , and when the notes in question get closer to that end , they become quieter . By the time our brain can even start to notice this , other , louder and lower notes have taken their place . That 's a weird and not very scientific explanation , but I hope it helps . URL_0"]
chatgpt_answers:
["In the song you shared, it sounds like the pitch is rising because the notes are arranged in a sequence that goes upward. But no single note is actually changing to a higher pitch. What’s changing is the pattern of the notes, and your brain hears that pattern as an increase.

Think of a staircase with ten steps, where each step stands for a different pitch. If you walk from the bottom step to the top, it feels like you’re going higher and higher. The steps themselves aren’t moving, though — you’re just stepping through them in order. Music can do the same thing.

So notes can act like stairs: when they’re played in rising order, your ear hears a climb in pitch even if the notes themselves aren’t literally sliding upward."]

question: Please explain what is "Recommender system"
human_answers: ["A recommender system, or a recommendation system (sometimes replacing 'system' with a synonym such as platform or engine), is a subclass of information filtering system that provide suggestions for items that are most pertinent to a particular user. Typically, the suggestions refer to various decision-making processes, such as what product to purchase, what music to listen to, or what online news to read. Recommender systems are particularly useful when an individual needs to choose an item from a potentially overwhelming number of items that a service may offer.\nRecommender systems are used in a variety of areas, with commonly recognised examples taking the form of playlist generators for video and music services, product recommenders for online stores, or content recommenders for social media platforms and open web content recommenders. These systems can operate using a single input, like music, or multiple inputs within and across platforms like news, books and search queries. There are also popular recommender systems for specific topics like restaurants and online dating. Recommender systems have also been developed to explore research articles and experts, collaborators, and financial services."]

It is used in many fields, but it is best known as the mechanism behind music and video playlist creation, product suggestions in online shopping, and content recommendations on social media.

Recommender systems can be built in several different ways, including:

Collaborative filtering: This approach generates recommendations from the past behavior of a user group. It examines what people with similar tastes have liked before and suggests those same items to another user.

Content-based filtering: This approach depends on the attributes of the items themselves. It analyzes an item’s features and then recommends other items with similar characteristics.

Hybrid systems: These combine collaborative filtering and content-based filtering to generate recommendations.

Common uses include recommending products on e-commerce sites, suggesting movies on streaming platforms, and promoting content on social media. By tailoring suggestions and bringing forward items a user is likely to care about, recommender systems can improve the overall user experience."]

question: Please explain what is "Automated reasoning"
human_answers:
["In computer science, in particular in knowledge representation and reasoning and metalogic, the area of automated reasoning is dedicated to understanding different aspects of reasoning. The study of automated reasoning helps produce computer programs that allow computers to reason completely, or nearly completely, automatically. Although automated reasoning is considered a sub-field of artificial intelligence, it also has connections with theoretical computer science and philosophy.
The most developed subareas of automated reasoning are automated theorem proving (and the less automated but more pragmatic subfield of interactive theorem proving) and automated proof checking (viewed as guaranteed correct reasoning under fixed assumptions).[citation needed] Extensive work has also been done in reasoning by analogy using induction and abduction.
Other important topics include reasoning under uncertainty and non-monotonic reasoning. An important part of the uncertainty field is that of argumentation, where further constraints of minimality and consistency are applied on top of the more standard automated deduction. John Pollock's OSCAR system is an example of an automated argumentation system that is more specific than being just an automated theorem prover.
Tools and techniques of automated reasoning include the classical logics and calculi, fuzzy logic, Bayesian inference, reasoning with maximal entropy and many less formal ad hoc techniques."]

Its uses are broad, spanning theorem proving, diagnosis, planning, and natural language processing. It plays an important role in mathematics, computer science, engineering, and artificial intelligence, helping with tasks that would be too difficult or impractical for people to do manually.\n\nA variety of techniques are employed in automated reasoning, including rule-based systems, resolution-based systems, model-based systems, and hybrid systems that mix several approaches. These systems may rely on different logical formalisms, such as propositional logic, first-order logic, and higher-order logics, to represent knowledge and reason about it. In simple terms, automated reasoning is about giving a computer the ability to reason logically and make decisions from established facts or axioms. It is a powerful way to tackle complex problems and perform work that would be infeasible for humans to do by hand."]

question: Please explain what is "Information theory"
human_answers: ["Information theory is the scientific study of the quantification, storage, and communication of information. The field was originally established by the works of Harry Nyquist and Ralph Hartley, in the 1920s, and Claude Shannon in the 1940s.: vii  The field is at the intersection of probability theory, statistics, computer science, statistical mechanics, information engineering, and electrical engineering.\nA key measure in information theory is entropy. Entropy quantifies the amount of uncertainty involved in the value of a random variable or the outcome of a random process. For example, identifying the outcome of a fair coin flip (with two equally likely outcomes) provides less information (lower entropy) than specifying the outcome from a roll of a die (with six equally likely outcomes). Some other important measures in information theory are mutual information, channel capacity, error exponents, and relative entropy. Important sub-fields of information theory include source coding, algorithmic complexity theory, algorithmic information theory and information-theoretic security.\nApplications of fundamental topics of information theory include source coding/data compression (e.g. for ZIP files), and channel coding/error detection and correction (e.g. for DSL). Its impact has been crucial to the success of the Voyager missions to deep space, the invention of the compact disc, the feasibility of mobile phones and the development of the Internet. The theory has also found applications in other areas, including statistical inference, cryptography, neurobiology, perception, linguistics, the evolution and function of molecular codes (bioinformatics), thermal physics, molecular dynamics, quantum computing, black holes, information retrieval, intelligence gathering, plagiarism detection,[10] pattern recognition, anomaly detection[11] and even art creation."]
chatgpt_answers: ["Information theory is a mathematical field concerned with how information is sent, handled, and stored. Claude Shannon developed it in the 1940s to define information precisely and measure how much of it can pass through a channel. One of its key ideas is that information is linked to uncertainty: the harder an event is to predict, the more information its outcome carries. For instance, if you know a coin is fair, heads or tails are equally likely, so the result tells you almost nothing. But if you are unsure whether the coin is fair, the outcome is less predictable and therefore more informative. The theory uses entropy to describe uncertainty or randomness in a system, with greater uncertainty meaning higher entropy. It also defines mutual information, which measures how much one random variable tells you about another. Information theory is widely used in computer science, engineering, and statistics, particularly for building efficient communication systems, compressing data, analyzing statistical data, and studying the limits of computation."]

question: Please explain what is "Supervised learning"
human_answers: ["Supervised learning (SL) is a machine learning paradigm for problems where the available data consists of labelled examples, meaning that each data point contains features (covariates) and an associated label. The goal of supervised learning algorithms is learning a function that maps feature vectors (inputs) to labels (output), based on example input-output pairs. It infers a function from labeled training data consisting of a set of training examples. In supervised learning, each example is a pair consisting of an input object (typically a vector) and a desired output value (also called the supervisory signal). A supervised learning algorithm analyzes the training data and produces an inferred function, which can be used for mapping new examples. An optimal scenario will allow for the algorithm to correctly determine the class labels for unseen instances. This requires the learning algorithm to generalize from the training data to unseen situations in a \"reasonable\" way (see inductive bias). This statistical quality of an algorithm is measured through the so-called generalization error."]
chatgpt_answers: ["Supervised learning is a type of machine learning where a model is trained on labeled examples so it can learn to predict outcomes. The training data provides both the input and the correct answer, and the model’s job is to uncover the relationship between them in a way that lets it work on data it has never seen. For instance, to create a supervised learning system that predicts house prices from features like size and location, you would need a dataset of homes with known prices. While training, the model receives the input features, such as the home’s size and location, along with the matching label, which is the price. After training, it should be able to estimate the price of a house whose actual value is unknown. Supervised learning is generally divided into two main categories: classification and regression. Classification is used when the output is a class, such as “cat” or “dog,” whereas regression is used for continuous outputs, like a house price. In brief, supervised learning relies on labeled data to teach a model how to predict results for new, unseen examples, and it includes both classification and regression problems."]

question: Please explain what is "Statistical classification"
human_answers:
["In statistics, classification is the problem of identifying which of a set of categories (sub-populations) an observation (or observations) belongs to.  Examples are assigning a given email to the "spam" or "non-spam" class, and assigning a diagnosis to a given patient based on observed characteristics of the patient (sex, blood pressure, presence or absence of certain symptoms, etc.).
Often, the individual observations are analyzed into a set of quantifiable properties, known variously as explanatory variables or features.  These properties may variously be categorical (e.g. "A", "B", "AB" or "O", for blood type), ordinal (e.g. "large", "medium" or "small"), integer-valued (e.g. the number of occurrences of a particular word in an email) or real-valued (e.g. a measurement of blood pressure). Other classifiers work by comparing observations to previous observations by means of a similarity or distance function.
An algorithm that implements classification, especially in a concrete implementation, is known as a classifier.  The term "classifier" sometimes also refers to the mathematical function, implemented by a classification algorithm, that maps input data to a category.
Terminology across fields is quite varied. In statistics, where classification is often done with logistic regression or a similar procedure, the properties of observations are termed explanatory variables (or independent variables, regressors, etc.), and the categories to be predicted are known as outcomes, which are considered to be possible values of the dependent variable.  In machine learning, the observations are often known as instances, the explanatory variables are termed features (grouped into a feature vector), and the possible categories to be predicted are classes.  Other fields may use different terminology: e.g. in community ecology, the term "classification" normally refers to cluster analysis."]
chatgpt_answers: ["Statistical classification is a supervised learning approach in machine learning and statistics where a model is trained on labeled examples so it can assign class labels to new inputs. The aim is to learn a rule that predicts the class of an unseen observation from one or more features. To accomplish this, a training set is gathered in which every sample is paired with its correct label. These examples are then used to construct a classifier, a mathematical model that translates inputs into labels. Once training is complete, the classifier can infer the class of data it has not encountered before by looking at its features. This task can be handled by many algorithms, including logistic regression, decision trees, and support vector machines. Which one is most suitable depends on the characteristics of the data and the performance level needed from the classifier. Statistical classification is widely applied in areas such as spam filtering, image classification, and credit fraud detection."]

question: Please explain what is "Sample complexity"
human_answers:
["The sample complexity of a machine learning algorithm represents the number of training-samples that it needs in order to successfully learn a target function.
More precisely, the sample complexity is the number of training-samples that we need to supply to the algorithm, so that the function returned by the algorithm is within an arbitrarily small error of the best possible function, with probability arbitrarily close to 1.
There are two variants of sample complexity:
The No free lunch theorem, discussed below, proves that, in general, the strong sample complexity is infinite, i.e. that there is no algorithm that can learn the globally-optimal target function using a finite number of training samples.
However, if we are only interested in a particular class of target functions (e.g, only linear functions) then the sample complexity is finite, and it depends linearly on the VC dimension on the class of target functions."]
chatgpt_answers: ["Sample complexity is the amount of training data, or the number of examples, that a machine learning algorithm needs before it can learn a task well or make reliable predictions. In other words, it describes how much data is needed for the algorithm to reach a particular level of performance. As a rule, harder tasks, or data that is noisier and more variable, demand more samples for accurate learning. On the other hand, when the task is straightforward or the data is clean and well structured, the algorithm may require only a small set of examples. This concept is important in machine learning design and use because it strongly affects both efficiency and effectiveness. For instance, an algorithm with low sample complexity can learn effectively from a small dataset and do so quickly, while one with high sample complexity may need much more data and a longer time to learn the task."]

question: Please explain what is "Intractability (complexity)"
human_answers: ["In theoretical computer science and mathematics, computational complexity theory focuses on classifying computational problems according to their resource usage, and relating these classes to each other. A computational problem is a task solved by a computer. A computation problem is solvable by mechanical application of mathematical steps, such as an algorithm.\nA problem is regarded as inherently difficult if its solution requires significant resources, whatever the algorithm used. The theory formalizes this intuition, by introducing mathematical models of computation to study these problems and quantifying their computational complexity, i.e., the amount of resources needed to solve them, such as time and storage. Other measures of complexity are also used, such as the amount of communication (used in communication complexity), the number of gates in a circuit (used in circuit complexity) and the number of processors (used in parallel computing). One of the roles of computational complexity theory is to determine the practical limits on what computers can and cannot do. The P versus NP problem, one of the seven Millennium Prize Problems, is dedicated to the field of computational complexity.\nClosely related fields in theoretical computer science are analysis of algorithms and computability theory. A key distinction between analysis of algorithms and computational complexity theory is that the former is devoted to analyzing the amount of resources needed by a particular algorithm to solve a problem, whereas the latter asks a more general question about all possible algorithms that could be used to solve the same problem. More precisely, computational complexity theory tries to classify problems that can or cannot be solved with appropriately restricted resources. In turn, imposing restrictions on the available resources is what distinguishes computational complexity from computability theory: the latter theory asks what kinds of problems can, in principle, be solved algorithmically."]
chatgpt_answers: ["Intractability, also called complexity, refers to how hard it is for a computer algorithm to solve a problem or carry out a task. Some problems are straightforward and can be finished quickly, while others demand far more effort and may take an extremely long time, or even be beyond what current technology can manage. This difficulty may come from the problem’s size, the number of steps needed to solve it, or the amount of resources it uses, such as time and memory. It can also stem from the problem’s inherent complexity, for example when it requires heavy computation or is not well defined. Common examples of intractable problems include the traveling salesman problem, the shortest path problem, and the knapsack problem. These are viewed as hard because they involve substantial computation or because there are many possible solutions to consider. Intractability is a key idea in computer science and strongly affects the way algorithms and computer systems are built and applied. Knowing a problem’s complexity helps researchers and engineers judge whether it can realistically be solved with current technology and encourages the creation of new algorithms and methods for tackling difficult problems."]

question: Please explain what is "Pruning (algorithm)"

question: Please explain what is "Genetic programming"
human_answers:
["In artificial intelligence, genetic programming (GP) is a technique of evolving programs, starting from a population of unfit (usually random) programs, fit for a particular task by applying operations analogous to natural genetic processes to the population of programs.
The operations are: selection of the fittest programs for reproduction (crossover) and mutation according to a predefined fitness measure, usually proficiency at the desired task.  The crossover operation involves swapping random parts of selected pairs (parents) to produce new and different offspring that become part of the new generation of programs.  Mutation involves substitution of some random part of a program with some other random part of a program. Some programs not selected for reproduction are copied from the current generation to the new generation. Then the selection and other operations are recursively applied to the new generation of programs.
Typically, members of each new generation are on average more fit than the members of the previous generation, and the best-of-generation program is often better than the best-of-generation programs from previous generations.  Termination of the evolution usually occurs when some individual program reaches a predefined proficiency or fitness level.
It may and often does happen that a particular run of the algorithm results in premature convergence to some local maximum which is not a globally optimal or even good solution.  Multiple runs (dozens to hundreds) are usually necessary to produce a very good result.  It may also be necessary to have a large starting population size and variability of the individuals to avoid pathologies."]

question: Please explain what is "Description logic"

question: Please explain what is "Kernel methods"
human_answers:
["In machine learning, kernel machines are a class of algorithms for pattern analysis, whose best known member is the support-vector machine (SVM). The general task of pattern analysis is to find and study general types of relations (for example clusters, rankings, principal components, correlations, classifications) in datasets. For many algorithms that solve these tasks, the data in raw representation have to be explicitly transformed into feature vector representations via a user-specified feature map: in contrast, kernel methods require only a user-specified kernel, i.e., a similarity function over all pairs of data points computed using Inner products. The feature map in kernel machines is infinite dimensional but only requires a finite dimensional matrix from user-input according to the Representer theorem. Kernel machines are slow to compute for datasets larger than a couple of thousand examples without parallel processing. 
Kernel methods  owe their name to the use of kernel functions, which enable them to operate in a high-dimensional, implicit feature space without ever computing the coordinates of the data in that space, but rather by simply computing the inner products between the images of all pairs of data in the feature space.  This operation is often computationally cheaper than the explicit computation of the coordinates.  This approach is called the "kernel trick". Kernel functions have been introduced for sequence data, graphs, text, images, as well as vectors.
Algorithms capable of operating with kernels include the kernel perceptron, support-vector machines (SVM), Gaussian processes, principal components analysis (PCA), canonical correlation analysis, ridge regression, spectral clustering, linear adaptive filters and many others.
Most kernel algorithms are based on convex optimization or eigenproblems and are statistically well-founded. Typically, their statistical properties are analyzed using statistical learning theory (for example, using Rademacher complexity)."]

A major benefit of kernel methods is their ability to handle data that is not linearly separable in its original representation. Once the data is mapped into a higher-dimensional space, a linear separation between classes may become possible. question: Please explain what is "Naive Bayes classifier"
human_answers: ["In statistics, naive Bayes classifiers are a family of simple \"probabilistic classifiers\" based on applying Bayes' theorem with strong (naive) independence assumptions between the features (see Bayes classifier). They are among the simplest Bayesian network models, but coupled with kernel density estimation, they can achieve high accuracy levels.\nNaive Bayes classifiers are highly scalable, requiring a number of parameters linear in the number of variables (features/predictors) in a learning problem. Maximum-likelihood training can be done by evaluating a closed-form expression,: 718  which takes linear time, rather than by expensive iterative approximation as used for many other types of classifiers.\nIn the statistics literature, naive Bayes models are known under a variety of names, including simple Bayes and independence Bayes. All these names reference the use of Bayes' theorem in the classifier's decision rule, but naive Bayes is not (necessarily) a Bayesian method."]
chatgpt_answers: ["A Naive Bayes classifier is a machine learning method used for classification problems. It relies on Bayes’ theorem, a statistical rule that gives the probability of an event under certain conditions. For classification, it looks at a sample’s features or attributes and estimates which class or label is most probable. The word “naive” comes from the assumption that every feature is independent of the others. This is a simplifying assumption that makes the approach quicker and more efficient, even though it is not always accurate. Naive Bayes is commonly applied in natural language processing, spam filtering, and other cases where feature independence is a fair approximation. It is a straightforward and effective algorithm that is easy to implement and scales well to large datasets. Still, for some kinds of data, it can do worse than more advanced methods."]

question: Please explain what is "Receptive field"
human_answers:
["The receptive field, or sensory space, is a delimited medium where some physiological stimuli can evoke a sensory neuronal response in specific organisms.
Complexity of the receptive field ranges from the unidimensional chemical structure of odorants to the multidimensional spacetime of human visual field, through the bidimensional skin surface, being a receptive field for touch perception. Receptive fields can positively or negatively alter the membrane potential with or without affecting the rate of action potentials.
A sensory space can be dependent of an animal's location. For a particular sound wave traveling in an appropriate transmission medium, by means of sound localization, an auditory space would amount to a reference system that continuously shifts as the animal moves (taking into consideration the space inside the ears as well). Conversely, receptive fields can be largely independent of the animal's location, as in the case of place cells. A sensory space can also map into a particular region on an animal's body. For example, it could be a hair in the cochlea or a piece of skin, retina, or tongue or other part of an animal's body. Receptive fields have been identified for neurons of the auditory system, the somatosensory system, and the visual system.
The term receptive field was first used by Sherrington in 1906 to describe the area of skin from which a scratch reflex could be elicited in a dog. In 1938, Hartline started to apply the term to single neurons, this time from the frog retina.
This concept of receptive fields can be extended further up the nervous system. If many sensory receptors all form synapses with a single cell further up, they collectively form the receptive field of that cell. For example, the receptive field of a ganglion cell in the retina of the eye is composed of input from all of the photoreceptors which synapse with it, and a group of ganglion cells in turn forms the receptive field for a cell in the brain. This process is called convergence.
Receptive fields have been used in modern artificial deep neural networks that work with local operations."]
chatgpt_answers: ["In computer vision and image processing, a neuron’s receptive field is the part of the input image that it can “see” or draw information from. In a convolutional neural network (CNN), the receptive field of a neuron in the first convolutional layer is set by that layer’s kernel size and stride. As the image moves through deeper layers and more kernels are applied, each neuron links to a larger area of the original image, so its receptive field expands. The size and form of the receptive field can strongly affect performance, because they control how much context the neuron can use when producing a prediction. A bigger receptive field lets the neuron take in more of the image, which is helpful in tasks like object recognition where interactions between different regions are important. At the same time, enlarging the receptive field increases computation and can raise the chance of overfitting, so it needs to be chosen based on the task."]

question: Please explain what is "AI accelerator"

Because these jobs are typically very demanding computationally and need substantial processing capacity, an AI accelerator shortens execution time by supplying specialized resources tailored to them.

Common examples of AI accelerators include: Graphics processing units (GPUs): processors originally created for the intensive calculations behind graphics rendering, now broadly used to accelerate AI and ML tasks. Tensor processing units (TPUs): custom Google chips developed to boost machine learning performance, including training and inference for deep neural networks. question: Please explain what is "Self-driving cars"

Self-driving cars use sensors to perceive their surroundings, such as optical and thermographic cameras, radar, lidar, ultrasound/sonar, GPS, odometry and inertial measurement units. Control systems interpret sensory information to create a three-dimensional model of the surroundings. Based on the model, the car identifies appropriate navigation paths, and strategies for managing traffic controls (stop signs, etc.) and obstacles.[10][11]
Once the technology matures, autonomous vehicles are predicted to impact the automobile industry, health, welfare, urban planning, traffic, insurance, labor market and other fields. 
Autonomy in vehicles is often divided into six levels,[12] according to a system developed by SAE International (SAE J3016).[13] The SAE levels can be roughly understood as Level 0 – no automation; Level 1 – hands on/shared control; Level 2 – hands off; Level 3 – eyes off; Level 4 – mind off, and Level 5 – steering wheel optional.
As of December 2022[update], vehicles operating at Level 3 and above were an insignificant market factor. In December 2020, Waymo became the first service provider to offer driver-less taxi rides to the general public, in a part of Phoenix, Arizona. In March 2021, Honda was the first manufacturer to sell a legally approved Level 3 car.[14][15][16] Nuro began autonomous commercial delivery operations in California in 2021.[17] In December 2021, Mercedes-Benz received approval for a Level 3 car.[18] In February 2022, Cruise became the second service provider to offer driver-less taxi rides to the general public, in San Francisco.[19]
As of December 2022[update], several manufacturers had scaled back plans for self-driving technology, including Ford and Volkswagen.[20]"]

question: Please explain what is "Garry Kasparov"

He formed the United Civil Front movement and joined as a member of The Other Russia, a coalition opposing the administration and policies of Vladimir Putin. In 2008, he announced an intention to run as a candidate in that year's Russian presidential race, but after encountering logistical problems in his campaign, for which he blamed "official obstruction", he withdrew.
